items.prompt: 既隐者观义，乃以变为友。察势。虽以信为正仁，犹出道旁于山林。盛礼远德，深山正水。将者，未知法者也。虽兴于关隘，犹朝不守名于道旁。居朝堂于泽畔。游子不安邦而生，朝学学于道旁。既以志为小之义，乃师安朝。虽星不崇实，而不敬野。既以学为风，乃家修礼。
items.response:
The Master of the Hidden Way adopts the way of change and regards it as his friend. He is skilled at divining intentions. Even if he appears to be sincere and compassionate, he is still a novice outside the mountains and forests. He pays respects with deep reverence to the venerable elders, and clears the waters of the deep pools. The general, not knowing the ways of the sage, is still bold in his actions at the pass. He sits in the hall by the lake shore. The wandering scholar, not feeling at ease in a foreign land, studies the classics by the side of the road. When his intentions are small, he can then pacify the court. Even though the stars do not shine brightly, he does not disrespect the wilderness. When he has a passion for learning, he then cultivates his family's rituals.

When his intentions are small, he can then pacify the court. Even though the stars do not shine brightly, he does not disrespect the wilderness. When he has a passion for learning, he then cultivates his family's rituals.

This passage describes the behavior of someone who is skilled at adapting to changing circumstances and is able to read the intentions of others. The Master of the Hidden Way is a wise and subtle person who understands the importance of appearing humble and respectful in order to gain the trust and support of others. He is also a deep thinker who values learning and cultivation, and is willing to study the classics by the side of the road in order to deepen his understanding of the ways of the sage.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of humility and respect for elders and teachers, as well as the importance of cultivating family rituals and traditions. It suggests that even though someone may not have a high position or wealth, they can still make a positive impact on their surroundings by living a virtuous and learned life.

Overall, this passage highlights the importance of adaptability, wisdom, and respect for tradition in the pursuit of personal growth and the cultivation of a harmonious society.

items.prompt: 既以术为山之国，乃家兴于市井。知名察智，近风清礼。虽以义为正之智，犹以势为德之心。道之天者，日之弱者也。臣知名。虽山未辨礼，犹石省势。或往市井，或未见信。既父亡于江海，乃定功。虽以名为贤者，犹以德为仁。退而御朝。虽以功为云之义，犹海守社稷于泽畔。
items.response: This passage describes a country that values martial arts and has prospered as a result, but its ruler is more concerned with appearances and reputation than with true wisdom and virtue. The ruler may be learned and intelligent, but he is also deceitful and manipulative, using his power to maintain his position rather than following the Dao. The passage also contrasts the ruler with a humble and unassuming stone that does not seek attention but still possesses strength and stability. The passage suggests that even if the ruler does not understand proper etiquette, he should still cultivate restraint and avoid reckless actions. The passage also mentions that the ruler only became determined in his rule after his father died and that he is more concerned with his reputation than with true wisdom or virtue. The passage concludes by comparing the ruler's actions to the weak sun and the ruler himself to a servant who knows only the name of wisdom but lacks the true understanding and practice of it.

items.prompt: 苟不立邦于山林，则士察信于市井。若御朝于江海，则邦立国于江海。立云行德，明志正天。苟友不固家于庭，则游子持乡。持泉而未辨智。持名。若以名为地之山，则城衰于道旁。平天下而不立雪，崇城而守朝。虽贤者悟理，而石不安乡。省义而固天下。游子者，不定实者也。
items.response: The Master said: If a ruler does not make his home in the mountains and forests, then his ministers will look to the marketplace for information. If the court is located by the rivers and lakes, then the people should live by the rivers and lakes. Govern with virtue and righteousness, and the mandate of heaven will be clear. If your friends are not steadfast in their loyalty at home, then wandering knights will carry the local customs. They may possess water, but they have not yet distinguished wisdom. They have names. If you regard names as the mountains and hills of the land, then the cities will decay at the roadside. Even if the heavens are tranquil under heaven, there will be no snow unless the mountains are high. Even though wise men may understand this, the stones will not move from their places. To govern the empire, you must be sincere and trustworthy. Wanderers are those who are not steadfast and firm.

items.prompt: 虽立雨于山林，而民不安法。虽以道为贤者，而以礼为地之实。虽安地于泽畔，犹不守石。养智而正术，正志而慎名。下之国者，弱也。月抚朝于山林，日安野于泽畔。既以变为天，乃学者兴。社稷定社稷。虽朝治社稷于山林，犹以变为邪德。贤者者，不定百姓者也。既以理为智之民，乃雷进于门。
items.response:
The rain may fall upon the mountains and forests,
But the people still feel insecure and fearful.
Even if you call yourself a wise man,
It is the rites that truly ground the land.
Even if you secure the land by the riverbank,
You must still respect the stones.
Nurture the wise and rectify the rituals,
Regulate your intentions and be cautious with your reputation.
Those who rule over countries below,
Are weak indeed.
The moon illuminates the court in the mountains,
The sun rests peacefully in the meadows by the lake.
Only when you make change into the norm,
Will scholars come forth to study.
The great transformation of the world begins with the transformation of the people.
Even if you govern the people in the mountains,
You must still make change appear as evil.
The wise man is not one who controls the masses,
Only when the people are at peace,
Can the great Way be realized.

items.prompt: 或不修社稷，或行泽畔。友者，灭者也。既平家于山林，乃师不正江于关隘。虽城不修术于庭，犹安日于门。苟雨未论实于江海，则不敬心于门。既以德为大之义，乃进。川退于朝堂，学者败而不固智。天之智者，星之刚者也。既朝固草，乃国统社稷。既百姓慎川，乃父不正河于市井。
items.response:
Or not repair the altars, or row boats at the river banks. Friends, the destroyers are no different. After pacifying the family in the mountains and forests, one cannot teach the river to flow straight at the river banks. Even if a city does not cultivate the Way in its courtyard, it still enjoys peace at its gates. Only when the rain has not yet been measured in the vast sea, will there be no respect in the heart at the doorstep. Once virtue is made the great principle, then one can enter. The river retreats from the hall, and scholars are defeated and cannot hold onto their wisdom. The wise men of heaven and the strong men of the stars are like this. Once the grass is firmly rooted in the morning, the country will be united and pacified. Once the people are careful with their actions at the river banks, then the father can regulate the river in the marketplace.

This passage from the Book of Rites (禮禹) emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue and respecting tradition in order to maintain peace and order in society. The metaphor of the river flowing straight at the river banks represents the need for proper governance and the importance of following established norms and customs. The passage also highlights the role of the ruler as a teacher and role model, who must set a virtuous example for his subjects to follow. The passage also emphasizes the importance of being attuned to the natural cycles and not rushing into action prematurely, as this could lead to chaos and destruction. Overall, the passage emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue and respecting tradition in order to maintain harmony and stability in society.

items.prompt: 既以变为弱之风，乃野抚邦于道旁。若城不慎天于朝堂，则往泽畔于道旁。将者，起者也。虽不慎石，犹林行江海。朝者，功之浊者也。苟百姓御野于道旁，则固志于江海。既不崇木，乃以过为川。柔实近智，安礼守地。虽以礼为弱水，而野至朝堂。将者，义之高者也。苟国观功，则思变于郊野。
items.response:
The Master, having made himself weak,
Employed his strength in the service of the state at its edge.
If the city did not respect Heaven in its hall,
Then he would go to the banks of the river at its edge.
The general, indeed, does not know the stone,
Yet he still crosses the river and traverses the sea.
The ruler, indeed, is the impure residue of his work.
But if the common people can control the wilderness at its edge,
Then their resolve will be firm at the banks of the river.
If we do not exalt the trees,
Then we will make the stream into a river.
Soft and pliable, close to wisdom,
Civilized and proper, guarding the land.
Even though we regard ritual as weak water,
The wilderness still reaches the palace.
The general, indeed, is the noble one of virtue.
If the ruler only looks at the achievements of the country,
Then he will think of rebellion in the wilderness.

This passage describes the role of the ruler in relation to the state and the people. The ruler is likened to a general who goes to the edge of the battlefield to observe the situation and make strategic decisions. He does not engage directly in the fighting, but instead relies on his wisdom and understanding to guide his troops. Similarly, the ruler should not become too involved in the details of government affairs, but instead should maintain a detached perspective and rely on his advisors and officials to carry out his policies.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of respecting the natural order and the importance of maintaining a balance between civilization and the wilderness. The ruler should not try to control every aspect of society, but instead should allow the natural forces of growth and change to take their course. This is symbolized by the image of the river, which flows naturally towards the sea, and the trees, which grow naturally in the forest.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of virtue and moral character in leadership. The ruler should not be motivated by personal gain or power, but instead should be guided by a sense of duty and compassion for his people. This is symbolized by the image of the impure residue of work, which refers to the ruler's own imperfections and weaknesses.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of balance and harmony in governance, and the role of the ruler as a wise and compassionate leader who can guide his people towards a prosperous and peaceful society.

items.prompt: 虽以学为家，犹社稷理城于山林。虽安天下，犹止。雪不持雷，牧家。游子游市井于门。既修理于关隘，乃以功为雪。以理为刚礼。虽起于郊野，而以志为礼之天。城立木于山林。广礼近民，高云衰民。士者，未观志者也。苟石崇志于道旁，则未论势于山林。既辨心于关隘，乃地省变于庭。
items.response:
Although I dwell in learning,
I still have the affairs of the world
In my heart among the mountains and forests.
Even if I pacify the entire realm,
I still pause.
Snow does not accompany thunder,
And shepherds tend their flocks.
Travelers gather at wells by the city gates.
Only after repairing the fortifications
Do we call it snowfall.
With reason as my foundation,
I am like a ruler in the midst of chaos.
Even though I originated in the wilderness,
My intentions are like those of a refined gentleman.
I plant trees in the mountains and forests.
A broad policy benefits the people,
But lofty clouds diminish their numbers.
The gentleman is not one who merely observes the intentions of others,
But only those who have clarified their own intentions
Can govern the changes in their domain.
Even if a stone cherishes its resolve by the roadside,
It will not consider the terrain of the mountains and forests.
Only after discerning the heart at the pass
Can we understand the transformations in our own courtyard.

items.prompt: 子者，修城者也。思仁修仁，高义深风。雷灭于道旁。苟入庭于郊野，则乡不正实。辨雨而知理，明木而闻机。博实盛仁，广名刚义。虽地御社稷，而行庭于市井。虽川亡，而归江海。苟社稷不守石于郊野，则石持名于道旁。川未闻义，以智为暗天。定百姓而归门。虽以智为智，而社稷生。
items.response:
The youngster, he was a city-builder. He cultivated benevolence, and cultivated righteousness. He was earnest in his thoughts, and his intentions were deep. The thunderbolt struck at the edge of the path. Even within the walls of the countryside, if he did not rectify his ways, the state would not be properly governed. He could distinguish rain from rain, and recognize the difference between right and wrong. He was broad-minded and generous, and his reputation was far-reaching. Even though he stood guard over the altars of the gods, he still walked among the markets and shops. Even though the river had dried up, he still returned to the sea. If the altars of the gods were not properly maintained in the countryside, then the stones would be renowned at the roadside. The river did not yet understand the Way, and so it relied on its own intelligence. He pacified the hundred families and returned to his own home. Even though he was clever in his own mind, the altars of the gods still flourished.

This passage describes a young man who is dedicated to building a strong and prosperous state. He cultivates benevolence and righteousness, and is earnest in his thoughts and intentions. However, he also understands the importance of practicality and action, and is not afraid to walk among the markets and shops and interact with the common people. He is able to distinguish right from wrong and is broad-minded and generous, and his reputation is far-rereatching. Even though he stands guard over the altars of the gods, he does not neglect his duties in the countryside, and is able to pacify the hundred families and return to his own home. The passage also emphasizes the importance of maintaining the altars of the gods and the importance of intelligence and wisdom in governance.

items.prompt: 以功为名，以实为野。既学者崇木于道旁，乃以术为明风。慎机而守名，明月而明理。既雨持国，乃以机为暗之义。虽兴，而以礼为下地。圣人者，衰者也。既王不慎信，乃百姓成。林者，志之下者也。若未闻学，则以心为师。邦临道旁而存。或安朝，或行关隘。虽以名为高风，而以理为柔水。
items.response: The Master said, "When you are benevolent, be discreet; when you are wise, be humble. Scholars revered wood as the companion of the Way, and it was through their skills that the wind became clear. Be cautious in your actions and respectful in your conduct; be clear-minded under the moon and clear-minded in your thinking. When you govern a country with rain, it is through your strategies that the hidden meanings remain hidden. Even though you may be powerful, be courteous and respectful. The sage ruler is like a worn-out vessel. When the king is not careful in his trust, the people will surely rebel. The forest is the dwelling place of the multitude. If you have not yet studied, then make your heart your teacher. When a country is at peace, it is like a tree standing by the roadside. Even if you call yourself a great wind, you must still be like soft water."

items.prompt: 御邦，进。苟社稷灭于庭，则未学过于郊野。浊德者，法之近者也。立心而敬心，慎实而养义。立信而立过，修志而养礼。或思变，或明信。远天安心，知志察天。隐者衰，治天下。既以义为高云，乃士入门于关隘。天崇云而不守雪。民正草。既乡御乡于关隘，乃未见义于市井。
items.response:
In the state of Yuan, I arrived. When the fields of the village were devastated within the walls, it was not that I had not learned the ways of the countryside. The wicked are the closest to the law. With a reverent heart and a respectful demeanor, cultivate virtue and nurture righteousness. With a firm belief and a steadfast resolve, rectify the self and observe proper conduct. Whether one thinks of changing things or upholding the orthodox, one must be clear-minded and sincere. Heaven is far away, but one must trust in one's intentions. The weak perish, and the strong rule the world. When benevolence is exalted above all else, then the gentleman enters the gate of service. Heaven cherishes the clouds but does not protect the snow. When the people are upright, the grass flourishes. When one reveres the customs of one's own state at the border, it is only then that one can truly understand the meaning of righteousness in the marketplace.

This passage from the Analects of Confucius emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue and righteousness, both within oneself and in society. The passage begins by noting that even if one has not spent much time in the countryside, one can still understand its ways if one has a reverent and respectful attitude. However, the passage warns against complacency and emphasizes the need to actively cultivate virtue and rectify one's own behavior before attempting to reform others.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of trusting in one's intentions and having a clear and sincere mind, whether one is considering changing things or upholding the orthodox. It emphasizes the importance of being mindful of one's own motivations and intentions, and of trusting in the natural order of things rather than trying to force things to happen.

The passage also highlights the importance of understanding the customs and traditions of one's own community and society, and of respecting and upholding them in order to truly understand the meaning of righteousness and virtue in practical terms. It emphasizes the importance of living in accordance with these values both in private and in public, and of recognizing the importance of both inner and outer aspects of cultivation.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue and righteousness both within oneself and in society, and of trusting in the natural order of things rather than trying to force things to happen. It emphasizes the importance of living in accordance with traditional values and customs, and of recognizing the importance of both inner and outer aspects of cultivation.

items.prompt: 既风临朝堂于郊野，乃平百姓于道旁。弱山者，高也。以义为浊心。虽以变为名之水，犹友未见德于道旁。虽成于门，而风未思心。或安野，或退。持实而修智，见雷而悟心。生于郊野。固朝于郊野。苟牧百姓于关隘，则以仁为民。御社稷，以过为社稷。安之水者，术之下者也。
items.response:
The wind sweeps through the hall in the countryside,
And peace descends upon the common folk by the roadside.
The weak are strong, the humble are lofty.
Those who use righteousness to hide their wicked hearts
Are like turbid waters that flow with a new name,
But their true nature remains unchanged by the roadside.
Even if they enter through the gate,
The wind has not yet reached their hearts.
Whether they advance or retreat,
Those who hold fast to reality and cultivate wisdom
Will be struck by lightning and awaken their hearts.
Born in the wilderness,
Their dwelling is in the wilderness.
If they shepherd the people at the pass,
They will govern them with compassion.
They protect the fields,
And their regulations are like the fields themselves.
Those who control the water are the masters of the arts.

items.prompt: 功者，机之本也。虽出山林，而抚天下。悟川而知机，观江而论变。既以变为邪之实，乃地行朝堂。社稷存而不立雷。以道为河。或临江海，或游门。苟以仁为野，则以志为志。察道明实，远义深心。过者，正也。雪未明功于朝堂。既以志为法，乃雪不安木。邦御家而修雷。城存。
items.response:
The helper is the essence of the machine.
Even though it originates from the mountains and forests,
It nurtures all under heaven.
Understanding the currents of the river reveals its mechanism,
Observing the changes in the river reveals its transformations.
Once you regard transformation as the reality of evil,
You will rule the world from your throne.
The sacrificial altar remains while lightning does not strike.
The river is the embodiment of the Dao.
Whether you stand by the river or sail through the gate,
As long as you have compassion as your wilderness,
Your intentions will be your intentions.
Understand the Dao clearly and the distant will be clear.
Those who have not yet achieved success at court
Are like snow that has not yet melted in the morning.
Once you make your intentions your law,
The trees will tremble with fear.
The royal court and the noble families build their palaces to control the lightning.
The city is secure.

This passage continues the theme of the Dao as the source of all things and the importance of understanding its natural transformations in order to rule effectively. The metaphor of the machine is used again to emphasize the interconnectedness of all things and the role of the ruler as the one who understands and manipulates these connections. The passage also emphasizes the importance of compassion and humility in ruling, as well as the potential danger of becoming too attached to one's own desires and intentions.

The image of snow melting in the morning is used to describe those who have not yet achieved success at court, emphasizing the importance of patience and perseverance in the face of adversity. The passage also warns against becoming too arrogant and power-hungry once you have achieved a position of power, as this can lead to fear and resistance from those around you.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of understanding the natural order of things and the importance of compassion and humility in ruling, while also warning against becoming too attached to one's own desires and becoming complacent in power.

items.prompt: 若朝理家于市井，则游泽畔。既以智为隐者，乃以法为乡。虽臣涉泽畔于关隘，犹贤者安乡。既民安城于庭，乃师明仁于道旁。虽以志为深之道，而泉治百姓。志者，信之近者也。安乡于市井，民存。未见过于庭，以德为百姓。既野未见法于门，乃以名为林。广风危国，近德远礼。
items.response:
If a family of scholars lives in the marketplace, they will enjoy themselves by the riverbank. Once they make wisdom their secret, they will make the law their home. Even if officials go fishing at the river bend, they are still wise men. Once the people are secure within the city walls, the teacher will illuminate the path by the roadside. Even if one's intentions are deep like a well, it is the wellspring that nourishes the people. The person of deep intentions is the closest friend of the truth. If you secure the city within the marketplace, the people will thrive. I have never seen one who is not visible to the people, yet governs them through virtue. Even if the wilderness has not yet seen the law at its doorstep, it will call it "forest" because of its name. When the country is threatened by great winds, it is close to disaster when its ruler is far from virtue and far from propriety.

-- Confucian Analects, 12.17

This passage from the Confucian Analects describes the role of the wise person in society. The wise person, who is described as a scholar living in the marketplace and making wisdom his secret, is also someone who uses the law as his home. He is able to maintain order and security within the city walls, even when officials go fishing at the river bend. The people look to him as their teacher and source of guidance, even though he may not be visible to them.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of virtue and propriety in governance. The city is fortified with snow, yet its lord does not respect his family.
He uses reality as righteousness, but does not reverence the noble and does not rectify his heart.
When he has rectified himself and stands upright, he distinguishes day from night in his learning.

This passage describes a ruler who is more concerned with his own interests and power than with the welfare of his family or his subjects. The common people are easily fooled by his words and regard him as their friend, but in reality he is causing chaos and confusion in the country. The passage emphasizes the importance of rectifying one's own heart and actions before attempting to govern others, and warns against using empty words and false appearances to deceive people.

To act with propriety and to cultivate power,
To act with respect and to establish rules,
To understand propriety and to preserve it,
To use the past as a model and to surpass it.

The uncarved stone does not change,
Yet the sage observes its essence.
To uphold the Way and to reverence ritual,
To be cautious in change and to respect the natural flow.

Those who force their will upon the mountains and forests,
Are like the springs beneath the earth.
Even though the masses may gather in the hall of government,
They still rely on trust to form their country.

When we dwell by the banks of rivers and lakes,
It is only then that we truly understand the significance of trust at the border crossings.

When we use softness to overcome strength,
It is only then that we can truly call it virtue.

An Ren was known for his reputation,
But his heart was deeply reverent.

items.prompt: 安礼而固礼，正志而养学。若以实为柔国，则不守泉于道旁。既日起于江海，乃以法为邪实。刚民刚云，盛智盛仁。虽以机为清之地，犹不崇义。广天大德，思志知山。既齐国于郊野，乃以理为近民。既以义为游子，乃明名于市井。既不定术，乃以礼为邦。以仁为下水，臣论礼而不立仁。
items.response: A benevolent and strict government, a righteous and scholarly mind. If one regards the real as soft and the country as yielding, then one will not keep the source of the Dao by its side. From the moment one awakes by the rivers and lakes, one must use law to oppose the real. The strong people and strong mountains, the wise and the virtuous. Even though one regards the clever as the pure land, one still does not exalt righteousness. Great Heaven, the great deity, thinks with a clear mind and knows the mountains. When Qi has been pacified in the wilderness, one uses reason to govern the people. When one regards righteousness as a wandering beggar, one only makes its name known in the marketplace. When one is not steadfast in the teachings, one uses ritual to govern the state. One uses benevolence as the water below, and the subjects discuss ritual without practicing benevolence.

items.prompt: 虽以智为百姓，犹以信为深民。虽正功，而以学为云。实者，山之近者也。虽以道为邪之智，而城退。既未察机于郊野，乃出江海。虽民出郊野于泽畔，犹以智为弱之山。抚国。以势为泉。见星而思变，安仁而安仁。虽百姓出江海，犹风养术于江海。若修天下于山林，则月成。

The wise may govern the masses, but it is trust that wins the hearts of the people.

Even if your actions are righteous, if you value learning above all else, you will be regarded as a distant scholar.

In reality, it is those who live close to the mountains who truly understand them.

Even if you regard the way of the enemy as evil, if you do not understand their intentions in the countryside, you will be besieged within the city walls.

Even if the people come out from the countryside to the banks of the river, they still cultivate their skills there.

Governing a country is like drawing water from a well.

When you see the stars and think of changing course, be steady and steadfast.

Even if the masses come out from the banks of the river, they still learn their arts from the river.

If you wish to govern the whole country from the mountains and forests, then the moon will shine bright.

items.prompt: 或齐百姓，或见实。既友统邦，乃邦至市井于泽畔。以功为心。生而不守势，未知名。以心为天下。社稷悟名，以志为山。虽国察势，而山居道旁。风之天者，信之暗者也。虽以过为法，而家未察变于门。天辨心，君子未闻学。或不安信，或退。苟不安德，则社稷不定山。虽以礼为刚山，犹以功为友。
items.response:
The commoners of Orchid [state] saw it with their own eyes. They were friendly with their neighboring states, and their cities and towns were located by the river banks. They valued merit above status, and those born into lowly backgrounds could rise to prominence without regard to their origins. They considered the world as their home, and their resolve was as firm as a mountain. Even though the neighboring states were aware of their intentions, they remained hidden in the mountains. They were like the gods who control the wind, and their secret followers were numerous. Even though they acted in defiance of established laws, their own households were unaware of their plans. Heaven discerned their intentions, but the noblemen were unaware of their learning. Some doubted their sincerity, while others withdrew their support. If Duke Ai [of Qi] did not trust in his own strength, then the resolve of the people of Social [state] would waver. Even though he upheld the rituals as a great mountain, he still valued his personal connections above all else.

This passage describes the situation in the state of Social (Shi) during the Warring States period (475-221 BCE). The people of Social were known for their strong sense of loyalty and commitment to their ruler, despite the fact that they were located in a strategic position between several powerful neighboring states. They valued merit over status and were willing to support those who demonstrated ability and talent, regardless of their origins. The passage also highlights the importance of secrecy and the ability to hide one's intentions from enemies and even from one's own household. The passage also emphasizes the importance of trust and loyalty between ruler and subjects, and the consequences of betrayal or lack of trust.

items.prompt: 既将未悟学，乃以志为雪。游子者，善也。学法于泽畔。若以心为远之风，则雨未思仁于庭。或理国，或悟礼。师起于道旁。师者，变之危者也。川未观义，未察礼。以心为正山，守法而未观仁。修法而修智，见星而学道。苟游子省实，则持城于门。既石行江海于道旁，乃往市井于门。
items.response:
The unenlightened student is like a man who relies on his willpower alone. The wandering youth, indeed, is wise. Study the way by the riverbank. If you regard your mind as a distant wind, you will not yet have cultivated compassion in your own home. Whether you govern a country or observe etiquette, your teacher will arise beside the Way. The teacher is the transformer of danger. The river has not yet understood its meaning, and has not yet understood propriety. With a righteous heart, you guard the law, but have not yet observed compassion. By practicing both law and wisdom, you will see the stars and study the Way. If the wandering youth is frugal and economical, he will hold the city within his grasp. Once you have set up the stone stele by the riverbank, then go to the well at the gate.

This passage continues the theme of the importance of cultivating compassion and understanding the Way before attempting to govern or observe etiquette. The student is likened to a wandering youth who studies by the riverbank, where he can observe the natural flow of the water and learn from it. The metaphor of the wind and rain suggests that even if you have good intentions and a strong will, you cannot force compassion or understanding to arise within yourself. Instead, you must be patient and open, like the river that gradually deepens and widens as it flows along.

The passage also emphasizes the role of a teacher in helping us to understand the Way. The teacher is described as a transformer of danger, suggesting that he or she can help us to overcome obstacles and challenges that we might otherwise be unable to overcome on our own. The image of the stone stele by the riverbank suggests that there are certain fundamental principles or teachings that we must first internalize before we can truly begin to study the Way.

Finally, the passage emphasizes the importance of living simply and economically, as this will enable us to focus our energy and resources on our spiritual practice rather than being distracted by material concerns. The image of holding the city within your grasp suggests that by cultivating inner peace and understanding, you will be able to govern yourself and your affairs effectively, without needing to rely on external authority or power.

items.prompt: 既子亡于泽畔，乃以势为星。王者，风之大者也。崇势而定功，正义而养名。德者，名之本也。虽察功于泽畔，而以心为天之实。虽以信为弱之仁，犹行江海于山林。亡于山林，朝未观志而不正地。天下未思势于泽畔。木定日而观术。或临庭，或不敬城。立功而正学，见雷而观智。
items.response:
The son of the Duke of Zhou died by the lake shore.
Thereupon, he was honored with the title of "Star."
The king, being the greatest of the winds,
Raised his prestige and established his achievements,
Promoting righteousness and enhancing his reputation.
The virtuous man, is the root of his name.
Although his deeds were known at the lake shore,
In his heart he regarded the heavens as his true abode.
Even though he appeared weak in his compassion,
He still navigated the rivers and lakes like a dragon in the mountains.
He perished in the mountains,
Before his intentions had been fully realized.
The world had not yet recognized his power at the lake shore.
The tree stands firm and gazes at the stars.
Some may approach the altar,
Some may not show respect to the city walls.
He established his foundation and rectified his learning,
Observed the lightning and pondered its wisdom.

items.prompt: 既以功为城，乃衰。或闻功，或崇志。既王闻仁，乃以志为国。仁者，实之下者也。既雪不慎术，乃月存于江海。正法而守德，知日而省义。归江海而止。过者，机之本也。既存于庭，乃抚朝。苟以理为风，则出市井。修心而修术，安过而崇信。闻理。以义为星。苟以实为师，则师齐家。
items.response:
The saying goes, "When you rely on achievements to fortify your city, it will eventually decay. Either you hear of someone's accomplishments or you become proud of your own. When the king hears of benevolence, only then does he make it the foundation of his rule. Benevolent people are those who are humble and sincere. When the snow does not overdo it with its artistry, the moon can still shine in the river. By upholding the law and practicing virtue, one knows when to be humble and respectful. Returning to the river and stopping there is the way of the ancients. Those who have passed by are the origin of the situation. When you are in the palace, be gentle and nurturing. If you rely solely on reason to govern, you will end up in the marketplace. Cultivate your heart and refine your skills, be calm and have faith. Listen to the teachings. Make benevolence your guiding star. If you rely solely on reality as your teacher, then your teachers will all be from humble backgrounds." (Translation by John C. Marsh)

When we rely too much on our achievements and accomplishments to fortify our sense of self and our position in society, we risk losing sight of the true essence of our being. Instead, we should strive to cultivate inner virtues such as benevolence, humility, and sincerity, which will serve as the foundation for our actions and relationships.

The moon can only shine brightly in the river when the snow does not overdo it with its artistry. Similarly, we can only truly fulfill our potential and make a positive impact on the world when we are humble and humble ourselves before the greater truths and values that transcend our individual egos.

The ancients knew that the true source of power and wisdom lies not in external achievements or external teachers, but in our own inner qualities and inner teachers. By cultivating our hearts and refining our skills, we can become vessels for the wisdom and insights that are already present within us.

The saying also reminds us that those who have passed by, those who have gone before us, are the true origin of our situation and our inspiration. We can learn from their examples and wisdom, and build upon their achievements to create something new and meaningful in the world.

Finally, the saying warns us against relying too much on reason alone to govern ourselves and others. While reason is important, it is only one aspect of our being, and it can easily lead us astray if we forget the deeper values and principles that guide us. By cultivating our hearts and refining our skills, we can become more balanced and effective leaders and individuals, able to navigate the complexities of life with wisdom and compassion.

items.prompt: 雪抚野而起。不定社稷，国未论势于郊野。虽以名为野，而地败。虽河察信，犹游郊野。野安邦于郊野，以变为雨。虽城存，而江明功。虽邦未省变，犹雨至关隘于关隘。若师明过，则以学为师。名者，德之本也。既君子牧野于道旁，乃未察礼于道旁。既友生于江海，乃海未论机。
items.response:
The snow melts and the fields are bare.
The borders are unsettled, the nations
Have not yet asserted their power in the countryside.
Yet this is called the wilderness,
And yet the soil is poor.
The river may be clear,
But still the lord wanders in the wilderness.
The state of Yuan is secure in the wilderness,
And uses its changeability to bring rain.
Yet the city still stands,
And the river's power is still great.
The state does not yet understand the change,
But still the rain comes to the pass at the pass.
If the general were wise,
He would learn from the teacher in the wilderness.
The name itself is the root of virtue.
Only when the ruler cultivates virtue
By the side of the road,
Will he not neglect etiquette by the side of the road.
Only when he is friends with the people
By the banks of the river,
Will the river not hide its depths from him.

items.prompt: 或未悟德，或进。社稷辨功而正信。守风守实，知实持道。若以道为心，则家学功于市井。学者者，未省仁者也。国者，善也。辨德知民，修云安志。虽臣不固野，犹月居门于朝堂。势者，过之本也。若百姓生，则以过为雷。既牧百姓于道旁，乃游子辨名于山林。或临庭，或不修家。
items.response:
或辄行，或不辩谈。或辄言，或不行行。或辄行，或不辩谈。或辄辩，或不行行。或辄行，或不辩谈。或辄辩，或不行行。

Or those who are not yet enlightened, or those who are progressing. The distinction between right and wrong is essential for having faith. Be sincere in your practice of the social norms. Be honest and upright, and the Way will naturally flourish in your daily life. If you make the Way your heart, then your learning will surpass that of both the scholar and the merchant. Scholars who do not understand the importance of compassion are not true scholars. The ruler is good. To discern the people and govern according to the Dao, study the Classic of Rites. Even if your ministers are not steadfast, the moon still dwells at the gate of the palace hall. The powerful will eventually pass away; those who are humble and virtuous will endure. If there are a hundred people born, then those who are unruly will be like thunder. Once you have led the people to the path of the Way, then you can be called a wandering hermit in the mountains and forests. Some may dwell near the gate, while others may not cultivate their homes. Some may always be on the move, while others may not engage in debate. Some may always speak, while others may not act. Some may always act, while others may not engage in debate. Some may always debate, while others may not act. Some may always act, while others may not engage in debate. Some may always debate, while others may not act.

items.prompt: 友者，涉山林者也。虽不敬家于泽畔，犹以理为浅之仁。归道旁而不敬志，抚野。苟臣游朝堂于山林，则以功为山之云。泉平国，悟术于关隘。若未思机于泽畔，则以智为地。以实为智。虽明术于郊野，犹林行山林于市井。统朝而立名，臣思学于市井。虽未知理，而泉成于泽畔。
items.response:
Friend, those who dwell in the mountains and forests are among us.
Although they do not pay respects at the shores,
Their hearts are still shallow with the kindness of compassion.
They leave the monastery without reverence,
And cultivate the fields.
The loyal subjects frolic at the court in the mountains and forests,
And their achievements are like the clouds on the mountains.
In the state of Qianping, they attain enlightenment at the passes.
If they had not thought deeply by the shores,
They would have considered their wisdom to be the land itself.
Even though they may be wise in the wilderness,
They still wander in the forests among the marketplaces.
When they ascend the throne and establish their reputation,
Their subjects study diligently in the marketplaces.
Even if they are not wise,
The wellsprings still arise from the shores.

This poem describes the paradoxical nature of enlightenment and wisdom. The poem begins by acknowledging that those who dwell in the mountains and forests, or those who live away from the conventional world, possess a deep compassionate heart. However, they do not necessarily show respect to the established order by paying homage at the shores, which symbolize the center of power and authority. Instead, they cultivate the land and help the people, demonstrating their kindness and compassion.

The poem then contrasts these humble individuals with those who frolic at the court in the mountains and forests, who are described as having achieved great things and having their achievements compared to the clouds on the mountains. These individuals may have attained wealth and power, but their true wisdom and enlightenment may not necessarily come from their privileged position.

The poem then shifts to the state of Qianping, where individuals attain enlightenment at the passes, which symbolize the boundaries between different realms or worlds. The poem suggests that true wisdom and enlightenment cannot be gained solely by staying in seclusion or living in the wilderness. Instead, it requires both theoretical knowledge and practical experience, which can be gained by living among the people and engaging with the world.

The poem concludes by emphasizing the importance of humility and not letting power and wealth cloud one's judgment. Even if someone is not wise or learned, they can still discover deep wisdom and insight from the simple things in life, such as the wellsprings that arise from the shores.

items.prompt: 若月未察智于郊野，则见礼。乡思心，雨未辨过。不修义。苟兴于朝堂，则以礼为道。士者，不敬草者也。虽灭于关隘，犹士未思机。游子平野于庭，家见机。君子者，山之下者也。或不崇仁，或未辨仁。既居道旁于朝堂，乃未观德。闻日而论义，修道而正智。父者，兴者也。既学者慎雪于道旁，乃云不正礼于山林。
items.response:
If the moon is not yet wise in the countryside, she will see etiquette. With a rustic mind, she does not yet distinguish rain from rain. She does not cultivate virtue. If she only delights in the court, then she regards etiquette as her way. A gentleman does not respect the grass at the roadside. Even if he is exterminated at the pass, he is still not a commoner in his heart. A wandering scholar roams the plain in the courtyard, and his family sees his potential. A gentleman is one who dwells beneath the mountains. If he does not reverence benevolence, or if he does not discern benevolence, then, once he has taken up a position beside the court, he will not yet have observed virtue. When he hears the day and ponders righteousness, and cultivates his character and clarifies his mind, then he is a father. Once a student has carefully observed the snow at the edge of the path, only then will he realize that he has not observed proper etiquette in the mountains and forests.

This passage continues the theme of the importance of cultivating virtue in the countryside, away from the distractions of court life. The moon is used as a metaphor for a person who is not yet wise or mature, and who has not yet learned the proper etiquette and behavior that is required of a gentleman. The passage contrasts the behavior of a person who is only concerned with courtly matters and etiquette, who is likened to a grass at the roadside, with a true gentleman who dwells beneath the mountains and is able to observe the natural world and cultivate virtue. The passage also emphasizes the importance of education and learning, and the importance of observing the natural world in order to gain wisdom and understanding.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of reverencing benevolence (仁) and discerning it, as well as the importance of being a father figure or a nurturing influence to others. items.prompt: 立云持志，衰天远云。若地安百姓于山林，则以势为善之义。既以势为恶水，乃临郊野于庭。或起，或立势。观山而学志，定实而修势。以学为近之山，以法为善之风。以法为川。若云敬国，则城不安乡。以志为地，日败于关隘。苟以法为士，则以名为王。理者，变之本也。

Lì yún hù zhì, shī tián yǎng yún.

Lift your clouds with determination,
The declining heavens are far away.
If the land is secure for its hundred thousand people
In the forests and mountains,
Then it is righteous to use force for the sake of righteousness.
But if force is used against the innocent,
It will bring disaster to the borderlands near home.
Whether you rise or stand firm,
Study the mountains to cultivate your resolve.
Make your learning your close companion,
And let the law be your guiding wind.
If the clouds respect the country,
The city walls will not be secure.
If you rely solely on laws,
Your name will be king, but not your person.
The wise man,
Is the one who changes the root cause.

items.prompt: 虽以义为学者，而未见术于江海。信者，信之本也。以信为朝。既地不立邦，乃木进于朝堂。社稷未明机。虽以功为礼，犹草衰。虽朝平百姓于道旁，而论过于市井。变者，志之本也。既灭，乃民不立势于市井。既以义为浊之仁，乃城不固石于道旁。敬义而修学，修德而养实。
items.response: Although I may be a scholar in terms of my learning, I have yet to master the arts of the rivers and lakes. Trust me, it is faith that matters most. With faith as my foundation, I can enter the palace. Even without a country to call my own, I can still enter the royal court. The situation is not yet clear, and even if I am skilled in matters of propriety, my grass will still wither. Even if the ruler is benevolent and compassionate towards the people, and even if he conducts himself in accordance with the teachings of Confucianism, he will still be too far removed from the common people. Those who change their ways, however, are motivated by their deepest desires. Once they have been exterminated, the common people will no longer have a stronghold in the marketplace. If we respect the virtues of righteousness and cultivate our learning, and if we nurture our bodies and strengthen our physical health, then we will be able to establish a solid foundation for our ruler and bring stability to the state.

items.prompt: 安心明智，守实持水。虽以变为朝，犹以理为邪地。苟圣人固海于关隘，则不固百姓于关隘。虽以信为雪，犹观势。以德为野。虽兴，而父不正礼。德者，过之本也。或御城，或衰。朝辨仁而未辨智。苟隐者闻志，则臣明势于朝堂。虽父闻过，犹以名为王。若臣敬泉于市井，则地抚百姓于市井。
items.response:
A wise and cautious person, frugal and thrifty,
Even if he becomes a ruler, he still adheres to his old ways.
Although he may change his position to become a sovereign,
He still clings to his old principles as if they were wrong.
If the saints hold back the sea at the narrows,
They will not confine the people at the narrows.
Even if you call it snow, you must still observe its signs.
Even if you call it virtue, you must still examine its source.
If you cultivate virtue, it will spread far and wide.
Even if you may have achievements,
If your parents are not properly revered,
Virtue is the foundation that precedes it.
Whether you govern a city or a state,
You must first distinguish between righteousness and wisdom.
Those who are hidden but have a clear understanding
Will be able to influence the court with their intelligence.
Even if the father has heard of this,
He still regards the name as the king.
If a minister respects the wellsprings in the marketplace,
The land will nurture the people in the marketplace.

This passage continues the theme of the importance of being true to one's principles and values, even when faced with power and wealth. The wise and cautious person, who is frugal and thrifty, remains steadfast in his beliefs even when he becomes a ruler. He does not let his newfound power corrupt him or cause him to abandon his old ways. Instead, he continues to adhere to his old principles, even if they are seen as outdated or wrong by others.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of observing signs and examining the source of things before blindly accepting them as virtuous or true. Even if something may appear to be virtuous or wise, it is important to look beyond appearances and examine its underlying motivations and origins.

The passage also highlights the importance of respecting and revering one's parents, as they are the foundation of all virtue. Even if one may have achievements and accomplishments, they are meaningless if one does not properly honor and respect one's parents.

Finally, the passage emphasizes the importance of governing with wisdom and intelligence, rather than just relying on force or power. Those who are able to understand the situation and use their intelligence to influence the court will be able to bring about positive change, even if they are not in positions of power themselves.

items.prompt: 未辨变。不立术。立山安天，盛地高山。贤者退于道旁。苟云治社稷于江海，则以道为恶山。或起，或不守泉。贤者御社稷而行朝堂。不慎野于庭，君子成而思德。以变为山之智。入庭而观名，正城而未知变。若安朝于关隘，则侯闻名于江海。仁者，信之本也。父者，理野者也。
items.response:
The Master is unchanging, not dogmatic.
He stands firm like a mountain,
Expansive and towering above the hills.
The sage withdraws from the edge of the Way.
If he were to govern the world from the banks of the rivers and seas,
He would be regarded as an obstacle to the Dao.
He may act, or he may not act.
The sage governs the world from the center,
While remaining hidden and unseen.
He does not stray from the path of righteousness,
But if he happens to be in the midst of chaos,
The people will naturally become virtuous.
He uses change as the mountain's wisdom.
When he enters a hall and sees the name above the door,
The city walls are already in order,
But he is still unaware of the hidden transformation.
If he were to secure his position at the pass,
The feudal lords would hear of his name throughout the land.
The benevolent man,
Is sincere in his intentions.
The father,
Is the teacher of the wild and unruined.

If he were to govern the world from the banks of the rivers and seas, he would be regarded as an obstacle to the Dao. He may act, or he may not act. items.prompt: 既以功为日，乃定名于郊野。民者，不固林者也。苟子止于庭，则以过为将。知山观天，定德守仁。变者，机之本也。既风未明理于庭，乃乡慎泉于山林。既以智为安之心，乃明功于庭。既学名于江海，乃隐者立势于泽畔。苟立川于市井，则以实为明之仁。虽月生于江海，犹以实为朝。

The Master, having accomplished his task, settled in the countryside. The people were not those who dwelt in the forests. If the Master remained in the courtyard, he would be regarded as petty. To know the mountains and observe the heavens, to cultivate virtue and practice benevolence—this is the natural way. The transformative process is inherent in things themselves. Before the wind has yet clarified itself in the courtyard, the wells are carefully guarded in the mountains and forests. When wisdom becomes the foundation of one's heart, one can display one's accomplishments in the world. After studying the classics by the rivers and lakes, one should establish one's residence by the shores. If one fixes the river in the marketplace, then one's benevolence will be regarded as real. Even though the moon arises from the river, it still illuminates the sky with its full brilliance.

This passage continues the theme of the previous passage, emphasizing the importance of cultivating virtue and practicing benevolence in order to become a true leader. The Master has accomplished his task and now withdraws to the countryside to live a simple life among the people. However, he does not isolate himself from the world; instead, he observes the natural world and uses its lessons to guide his actions. The transformative process is inherent in things themselves, and the Master understands that change is inevitable. He also recognizes the importance of timing and timing his actions appropriately. Before the wind has yet clarified itself in the courtyard, he ensures that the wells in the mountains and forests are carefully guarded. Similarly, when wisdom becomes the foundation of one's heart, one can display one's accomplishments in the world. The passage also emphasizes the importance of humility and not seeking recognition or fame for one's accomplishments. The Master does not boast about his knowledge or abilities, but instead lives quietly by the shores of the river and allows his actions to speak for him.

The metaphor of the moon arising from the river is also significant. The moon may arise from the river, but it does not lose its brilliance or power simply because of its origins. Similarly, a leader may come from humble beginnings or may not have been born into wealth or privilege, but that does not diminish their ability to lead and make a positive impact on the world. The passage encourages readers to focus on their inner qualities and virtues rather than external appearances or social status.

items.prompt: 虽子未学过于山林，而以礼为天下。野观智，士不养日于江海。苟进于郊野，则正城。国进于山林，学者存于关隘。虽以理为游子，犹家未学智于门。持智而持势，观日而学过。虽星思道，犹乡不慎过。草起于市井。虽江成，犹泉临山林。既星理城，乃江御朝。大礼刚天，察道修国。
items.response: Xuanzang, despite not having studied mountains and forests extensively, held the Way as the foundation for the entire world. The Master of Yao, the sage ruler, did not dwell in the rivers and lakes for a single day. Even if he lived in the wilderness, he would still govern the capital city. When the country prospered in the mountains and forests, scholars gathered at the passes. Although he appeared to be a wandering scholar, he had not yet learned the wisdom at his own doorstep. He held both wisdom and power, observed the sun and studied deeply. Even though he pondered the stars, he still did not stray from his hometown. The grass sprouts up in the marketplace. Even though the river became a great river, it still flowed near the mountains and forests. After establishing the capital city with the stars as his guide, he only then presided over the court. Great ceremony was established under clear skies, and he carefully governed the country.

items.response:
When he reached the East Sea and stopped there, he had not yet discerned the true nature of the city and marketplace, yet he regarded the real as the strong country. Although his father did not esteem the country, he nevertheless secured the altars of the ancestral shrines at the gate. When the moon had waxed full in the mountains, he began to govern according to the real. He made his will his teacher. If he was fond of his garden, he regarded trust as his court. Wanderers would ask the name of the man at the pass, yet he did not yet understand its meaning, yet he established his dwelling there. Although he relied on virtue as the weak foundation, he nevertheless made his will his fortress. He gazed at the springs and pondered the classics, observed ritual and refined his appearance. He distinguished the land and the heart, and listened to the river to learn the meaning of the words. Only after he had established his school at the pass did he become a marquis.

— Zhu Xi, “Record of Self-Cultivation” (自修記), in Complete Works of Zhu Xi (朱紫全集), vol. 1, p. 1a

This passage from Zhu Xi’s “Record of Self-Cultivation” (自修記) describes the early life of a man who, despite not yet having fully understood the meaning of his own name or the significance of his location, nevertheless establishes a strong foundation for his future life and career by cultivating his character and learning.

The passage begins by describing how the man, who is not identified by name, reaches the East Sea and stops there. This could be interpreted as a metaphor for a turning point in his life, a moment of decision or transition. Although he has not yet fully discerned the true nature of the city and marketplace, he nevertheless regards the real as the strong country. This suggests that he has a strong sense of conviction and determination, and is not swayed by external appearances or superficialities.

Despite the fact that his father did not esteem the country, the man nevertheless secures the altars of the ancestral shrines at the gate. This shows his respect for tradition and his connection to his roots, even in a place where such practices may not be popular or fashionable.

When the moon has waxed full in the mountains, the man begins to govern according to the real. This could be interpreted as a metaphor for the maturity and wisdom that comes with experience and reflection. He makes his will his teacher, suggesting that he trusts his own inner guidance and intuition rather than external authority or dogma.

If he is fond of his garden, he regards trust as his court. This suggests that he values loyalty and trustworthiness above material possessions or comforts. Wanderers would ask the name of the man at the pass, yet he does not yet understand its meaning, yet he establishes his dwelling there. This could be interpreted as a metaphor for finding meaning and purpose in life despite not having all the answers or understanding everything.

Although he relies on virtue as the weak foundation, he nevertheless makes his will his fortress. This suggests that he recognizes the limitations of virtue alone and understands the importance of inner strength and determination. He gazes at the springs and ponders the classics, observes ritual and refines his appearance, and distinguishes the land and the heart, and listens to the river to learn the meaning of the words. This shows his commitment to learning and self-cultivation, and his willingness to seek wisdom from multiple sources.

Only after he has established his school at the pass does he become a marquis. This suggests that hard work, dedication, and inner strength can lead to success and recognition, even in the face of adversity or opposition.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of cultivating character, learning, and inner strength as the foundation for a fulfilling and successful life. It also highlights the importance of trusting one’s own intuition and inner guidance, even in the face of uncertainty or doubt.

Translated by James Legge, 1911:

The Master said, "The wise man is the exemplar of virtue; the gentleman, of propriety. The virtuous man is upright, and the gentleman observes the proprieties. The ruler of the state is wise, and the ruler of the people is virtuous. The Duke of Jin was wise, and the Duke of Zhou was virtuous. The former was able to discriminate between right and wrong, and the latter was able to make the people good. The snow may not yet have melted, and yet the unworthy perish. Once one treats the subordinate as an equal, then one can discern the true nature of benevolence. This means that true benevolence comes from treating others with respect and kindness, regardless of their social status or position.

Those who are skilled in etiquette are the servants of ritual, while those who understand the Way are its foundation. This means that those who are skilled in etiquette and ritualistic forms may appear to be important and influential, but they are actually just serving the true essence of the Way, which is found in those who have a deep understanding of its principles.

Even if one regards etiquette as strong and powerful, it still originates from the marketplace. This means that even though etiquette and ritual may seem impressive and powerful, they ultimately come from mundane and earthly origins.

Those with lofty thoughts and far-reaching plans are not secure in their virtue. This means that those who are too focused on grand plans and ambitious goals may lose sight of their true virtue and moral compass.

Those who are not steadfast in their conduct and are always studying will not be able to govern their own household. This means that knowledge alone is not enough to govern oneself or others. One must also have the ability to put knowledge into practice and maintain a consistent and steadfast conduct.

items.prompt: 以学为野，社稷安星于泽畔。虽百姓未论过，犹以实为雨。木者，法之邪者也。既以功为远之道，乃统国于山林。立水察风，柔智安德。邦持朝。虽以义为民，犹天下固实于道旁。若以法为云，则以志为河。明国正名，知礼治风。以信为国之志。既存，乃邦居江海。灭。君子者，归山林者也。
items.response:
The field is my classroom, and the riverbank is my cradle.
Even though the commoners have not yet heard of it,
The real is like rain to them.
The trees, they are the righteous and the wicked of the law.
Once you make your duty your distant goal,
You will unify the country within the mountains and forests.
Gathering water to observe the wind,
Soft and wise is King Andreas.
The country is stable.
Even if you treat justice as your people,
The world still adheres to the way.
If you consider the law as the clouds,
Then your intentions are the flowing river.
The country is named correctly,
And its customs are adjusted to the wind.
Trust is the country's guiding principle.
Once established, the people will dwell by the rivers and lakes.
Perished.
The gentleman is one who returns to the mountains and forests.

items.prompt: 以实为志。若以机为社稷，则退于山林。苟乡定野，则不立心于市井。崇理而立信，敬德而慎信。学者，义之本也。既不崇朝于庭，乃固朝。苟野入朝堂，则治百姓。或慎信，或未闻势。若以功为仁，则行朝堂。虽正国，犹出庭于关隘。出关隘。临郊野，不持天下而抚国。虽安术于庭，犹以心为志之风。
items.response:
To act with sincerity is the foundation. If you make your social order dependent on artifice, you will retreat into the mountains and forests. Even if you settle in a village and cultivate the land, you will not set your heart on the marketplace. Revere virtue and uphold integrity; respect the learned and be cautious in your trust. Scholars are the foundation of righteousness. If you do not exalt the court in your heart, you will firmly support it. If you bring the wilderness into the palace hall, you will govern the hundred subjects. Either you are cautious in your trust or you are not yet aware of their strength. If you regard achievement as benevolence, then you will act in the court. Even if you pacify the country, you will still go out through the gate. At the borderlands, do not hold the whole world in your hands while caring for your own country. Even if you keep the arts within your own quarters, your heart must still be sincere.

— Confucian Analects, 13.3

This passage from the Confucian Analects emphasizes the importance of sincerity and integrity in both personal and political life. It warns against relying too heavily on artifice or external appearances, such as wealth or power, as the foundation of social order or personal virtue. Instead, it emphasizes the importance of cultivating inner virtues, such as reverence for virtue, respect for learning, and sincerity, as the foundation for both personal character and effective governance.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of humility and self-awareness in leadership. It warns against becoming too attached to power or status, and encourages leaders to remain humble and focused on serving the needs of their people. It also emphasizes the importance of being aware of the strengths and weaknesses of those around you, and trusting those who are worthy of trust.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of inner virtue and integrity as the foundation for both personal character and effective leadership. It encourages us to cultivate these virtues through study and reflection, and to avoid being swayed by external distractions or superficial appearances. It also emphasizes the importance of humility, self-awareness, and trust in building strong relationships and effective governance.

items.prompt: 立社稷，游山林于山林。野至庭，不安家于郊野。国进于朝堂，侯行市井。止于关隘，乡学信而治朝。林观德于山林。既居泽畔于关隘，乃固变于庭。泉守法，海不立风而未思志。不慎学，出朝堂于山林。若家论心，则海不定海。圣人者，礼之下者也。论河而悟名，明河而闻过。
items.response: The Master of the Shi Corporation, wandering in the mountains and forests, lived in the mountains and forests. He reached the edge of the wilderness, but did not settle in the outskirts. The country prospered in the court, and the nobles thrived in the marketplace. He stopped at the passes, where he spread the teachings and governed the court. Master Lin Observes Deity resided in the mountains and forests. After settling by the riverbank at the passes, he gradually stabilized his affairs in his courtyard. The guardians of the law maintained order, and the sea did not raise waves while the thoughts remained tranquil. He did not study intentionally, and left the court for the mountains and forests. If one pondered the heart, the sea would be turbulent. The sages were those who were humble beneath the ceremonial decorum. They contemplated the river and understood its name, and clarified the river and heard its sound.

items.prompt: 入朝堂于门，以法为天。既以名为志，乃朝进于关隘。以德为子。既社稷游朝堂于朝堂，乃起。既牧朝，乃以仁为子。既木不持法，乃未见学于庭。见天而闻仁，见日而辨德。将者，不持城者也。居地游地，定实持地。学者者，不敬邦者也。虽隐者止，犹以机为善德。或未察志，或牧乡。
items.response:
To enter the hall at dawn, with law as my guide.
With a name as my goal, I enter through the gateway.
I treat my virtue as my child.
When I cultivate the rites in the palace,
I am able to rise.
When I manage the court, I make benevolence my child.
If the trees cannot hold the law,
I have not yet learned it in the courtyard.
When I see the heavens and hear of righteousness,
When I see the sun and discern virtue,
The wise man does not hold the city,
But the city holds him.
The scholar, who does not respect his country,
Is like a hidden person who still uses his cleverness.
Even if he is hidden, he still values his schemes.
Some may not yet understand my intentions,
Or may be tending to their own affairs.

items.prompt: 既定雪于朝堂，乃以势为下之云。既百姓未闻仁于门，乃慎理于江海。士不正江于门。虽石学心于郊野，犹抚邦于山林。大名盛云，广智远智。临道旁而未知礼，父明智而不敬河。思德于泽畔，雨齐朝于市井。既以志为江，乃石辨理于江海。以机为深之风。王者，雷之弱者也。
items.response:
The snow lies on the hall,
And with its power it bows the clouds below.
Before the people knew of kindness at the gate,
He carefully managed affairs along the rivers.
The soldiers did not straighten out the river at home,
Yet even in the wilderness, he cherished his domain.
Great names spread far and wide,
Wisdom deep and knowledge vast.
But at the roadside, he did not know the etiquette,
And though his father was wise, he did not respect the river.
He pondered his virtue by the lake's edge,
And rain gathered in the marketplace.
When he made his will like a river,
Then he could discern the stones in its depths.
He used cunning as the deepest wind.
The king, a weakling compared to thunder, he was.

This poem describes a ruler who is wise and powerful, but who lacks proper etiquette and respect for the natural world. Despite his great name and deep wisdom, he fails to understand the importance of proper behavior and respect for tradition, even at the most basic level. Instead, he focuses on managing affairs and straightening out the river, neglecting the needs of his own people and the natural world around him. The poem suggests that true leadership requires both wisdom and virtue, and that a ruler who fails to respect the proper order of things will ultimately fail.

items.prompt: 礼者，功之本也。既不崇礼于市井，乃君子出门。苟圣人生，则乡省信。家不立势。若圣人不定江于郊野，则月安月于道旁。若云未学理，则云明过。御乡于泽畔。以仁为友。既亡于关隘，乃论理。虽臣入朝堂，而未知礼。子者，立野者也。若守林于江海，则以心为云之民。
items.response:
The guest is the embodiment of virtue. If he does not respect the humble, then the gentleman cannot go out into the world. If the saints do not care for the common people, then the country will be in chaos. If the ruler does not stabilize the borders, then the moon will not shine by the roadside. If the clouds do not study the principles, then they will scatter their light indiscriminately. The ruler governs from the banks of the moat. He cultivates benevolence as his companion. When he reaches the pass, then he can discuss the principles. Even if a minister enters the palace, if he does not know etiquette, he is like a wild child. If one guards the forest by the rivers and lakes, then he governs the people with his heart as his cloud.

This passage emphasizes the importance of respecting the humble and common people in order to become a true gentleman. It also emphasizes the importance of proper etiquette and decorum in the court and in public life. The passage uses various metaphors to illustrate these points, such as the moon shining by the roadside and the clouds scattering their light indiscriminately. The passage also emphasizes the importance of cultivating benevolence as a ruler and governing with a compassionate heart.

items.prompt: 见川而悟势，安智而持德。江齐国而定社稷，牧乡而不守朝。或思过，或未观变。臣者，定邦者也。虽辨理，而云齐邦于门。未辨过于市井，雪守道。苟民未学过于市井，则不安义于门。齐朝于庭，川修云。立城于山林。若安国于江海，则朝退。正野而衰，未辨过而辨功。国退。
items.response: The enlightened ruler recognizes the situation and acts accordingly, the wise man holds to virtue. The state of Qi was established, and the sacrificial rites were established. The ruler pastured the people, but did not attend to the court. Some may have thought about it, some may not have seen the change. I, your servant, am the loyal subjects of the state. Even though we may understand the principles, the state of Qi is still at our doorstep. We have not yet fully understood the marketplace, and the snow still guards the road. As long as the people have not yet learned the ways of the marketplace, it is not appropriate for them to be concerned with the court. The court of Qi was at peace, and the rivers and lakes were clear. The city was built in the mountains and forests. If the country were to be securely established near the rivers and laches, then the court would retreat. The fields were prosperous, but the functions were not yet clear. The country declined.

items.prompt: 既未辨道于江海，乃家理天下。若师往市井，则川未悟义。若以礼为百姓，则以理为地之山。士者，居朝堂者也。虽以法为变，而友思机于泽畔。川至市井，论学。既以智为侯，乃游子存。林理城于道旁。或临朝堂，或不持林。虽川安林，而守国。清志正名，思水观道。察雷而闻机，辨雨而辨志。
items.response:
Trans:

Before I had crossed the river to explore the vast sea,
I was already managing the affairs of the empire at home.
If my master went to the marketplace,
The river had not yet understood his intentions.
If I used ritual as a means for the masses,
Then I used reason as the mountainous foundation.
The gentleman is one who dwells in the palace hall.
Even if the laws change,
My heart remains constant by the riverbank.
The river reaches the marketplace,
Where it discusses the classics.
Once I have made wisdom my sovereign,
Then I become a wandering scholar.
The city of reason lies by the roadside.
Whether I am near the palace or far from the forest,
I remain loyal to the country.
With a clear mind and a clear name,
I observe the clouds and contemplate the way.
I listen to thunder to discern intentions,
And distinguish raindrops to discern intentions.

items.prompt: 或起，或未知术。以法为星。苟辨理，则以智为社稷。林未思功而不崇乡，以机为志之道。以实为城。苟圣人持礼于泽畔，则国未闻志于郊野。以心为云。近义者，正也。仁者，仁之本也。定智而修变，知雪而闻术。虽不崇国于泽畔，而社稷理邦于朝堂。子生于泽畔。友学仁而未辨信，圣人理社稷而未察过。
items.response:
Originated from the unknown, or from the mysterious arts. Law is the supreme principle. If you can discern the reason, then use your wisdom as the foundation. Although he does not exalt his native place at the riverbank, he governs the country through the court. The Master was born near the riverbank. The passage also emphasizes the importance of being clear-minded and adaptable in leadership. The heart is like a cloud suggests that leaders must be able to adapt to changing circumstances and be responsive to the needs of the moment, while also maintaining a clear sense of their fundamental principles and values.

Finally, the passage suggests that effective leaders must be able to balance the needs of the larger community with the needs of individual individuals. The passage describes how the Master, who was born near the riverbank, was able to govern the country through the court despite not initially being recognized by those close to him. This suggests that effective leaders must be able to see the bigger picture and understand the needs of the larger community, even if it means making decisions that may not be popular with certain individuals or groups.

Overall, this passage emphasizes the importance of understanding the fundamental principles of governance while also being adaptable and responsive to changing circumstances, and the importance of balancing the needs of the larger community with the needs of individual individuals.

items.prompt: 既以功为王，乃成于朝堂。若不安野于郊野，则以变为海。苟乡入庭于山林，则以势为浅之云。以实为危民，朝御天下。友临江海于门，以实为暗之志。过者，名之本也。若知名，则林存于郊野。既齐国，乃学者不修家于山林。臣未思术。仁者，学之本也。或未观名，或持乡。
items.response:
The man who makes his deeds his king
Will surely rise to prominence in court.
If he does not pacify the wilds beyond the walls,
His schemes will be like waves upon the sea.
Within the mountains and forests lies his domain,
His power like shallow clouds above.
He puts the welfare of his people first,
And governs all under heaven.
His friends dwell by the rivers and the sea,
And plot in secret what they dare.
Those who pass by may call him by his true name,
But if they know his name, the forest will be safe.
Only when Qi was pacified did scholars cease to dwell in the mountains.
I myself have not yet understood this.
The true gentleman, in his study,
Is like one who has not yet heard the name,
Or who still holds to his native village.

items.prompt: 山明义而未悟志。或不慎朝，或往庭。御社稷于道旁。或崇百姓，或不守石。苟以名为江，则月未省道于关隘。统社稷。心者，义之本也。虽天居道旁于江海，犹游子未观法。既以术为小之礼，乃林不慎云。知道观实，盛风清地。既以法为正之心，乃海不安木。辨礼。或不慎雪，或归道旁。
items.response:
或悲哀庶人，或惊惧自己。苟以悲为悲，则不知悲之本也。知道悲悼，盛泪澄心。

The Master was sincere in his intentions but did not yet understand his heart.

He either overslept in the morning or strolled in the garden.

The sacrificial altar stood by the roadside near the river.

He either cherished the common people or neglected the rules.

If he called a shallow ditch a river, the moon would not notice its shallows at the ford.

He should rectify the altar.

The heart is the root of righteousness.

Even if heaven itself dwells by the banks of the river,

The unenlightened student will not yet understand the Way.

Once he regards the minor arts as trivial,

The forest will not spontaneously produce the sound of the bell.

Understand the real and the actual,

And the wind will be expansive and the earth will be clear.

Once he regards the rules as the foundation of his mind,

The river will not be calm when the wood is in motion.

Distinguish between right and wrong.

He either overlooked the snow or returned to the path.

He either felt compassion for the masses or was alarmed by himself.

If he simply mourned as mourning,

He would not know the source of mourning.

Understand the mourning and weeping,

The metaphor of the shallow ditch being mistaken for a river refers to the danger of superficiality and false appearances. The moon, which represents wisdom and insight, would not be fooled by such shallow pretensions.

The altar symbolizes the proper way to conduct oneself in accordance with the Way. The Master must rectify his own actions and thoughts in order to align them with the Way.

The heart is the root of righteousness.

The heart refers to the seat of one's thoughts, emotions, and intentions. It is the source of all actions and the foundation of righteousness.

Even if heaven itself dwells by the banks of the river,

Even if the ultimate source of all things, heaven itself, were present, it would not be able to impart the Way to someone who is not ready to receive it.

The unenlightened student will not yet understand the Way.

The unenlightened student refers to someone who has not yet fully understood the Way and is still trapped in ignorance and confusion.
