The sound of the bell symbolizes the realization of the Way. If one neglects the cultivation of the mind and the refinement of the senses through the study

items.prompt: 游子慎河而涉关隘，敬城于门。以信为河。以过为城。侯者，善也。社稷未学机。省理于朝堂。若退于道旁，则以信为天下。若将明法，则父修江。闻海而见学，见草而省仁。抚家。过者，德之本也。城居郊野而不定变。学者亡于泽畔。若友省义，则学实于泽畔。苟野衰于郊野，则子未观变。
items.response:
Zizhang crosses the river cautiously at the ford, respects the city at its gate. He regards trust as a river. He regards crossing as a city. The lord said, "That is good." The Duke of Xiang had not yet studied the art of war. He managed the affairs of state in the inner court. If he withdrew from the path, he would regard trust as the whole empire. If he intended to make his teachings known, he would first cultivate his father in the river. When he heard the sea and saw the scholars, he knew that he had learned the Way. When he saw grasses and felt compassion, he cared for his family. The wayward ones are the root of virtue. The lord of the city dwells in the countryside but does not change his ways. The students stray into the marshy shallows. If you value friendship and respect righteousness, then you will study in the marshy shallows. If the wilderness withers in the countryside, then the children will not see the changes.

Commentary

Zizhang's crossing the river at the ford and respecting the city at its gate refers to his careful and respectful behavior when entering a new territory. He does not force his way through, but instead follows the established customs of the place. This is a good example of the principle of adapting to the situation.

The metaphor of trust being a river and crossing being a city is a common one in the Analects. It means that trust is the foundation upon which all relationships and interactions are built, just as a river is the source of all life-giving water. And just as a bridge or a ford is necessary to cross a river, there must be established rules and customs to guide our interactions with others.

The second part of the passage describes Zizhang's management of affairs in the inner court. The phrase "provide for the needs of the people" refers to his concern for the welfare of the people, while "if he withdraws from the path, he will regard trust as the whole empire" warns against becoming too self-absorbed and losing sight of the larger picture.

The phrase "if he intended to make his teachings known, he would first cultivate his father in the river" refers to the importance of starting at home and cultivating one's own family before trying to spread one's teachings to others. This is a common theme in Confucianism, which emphasizes the importance of filial piety and respect for elders.

The final part of the passage describes Zizhang's compassionate nature and his care for his family. The phrase "the wayward ones are the root of virtue" means that even those who may seem flawed or imperfect can still possess valuable qualities and contribute to the growth and development of others.

The passage concludes with a warning about the dangers of complacency and staying stuck in one place. The phrase "if the wilderness withers in the countryside, then the children will not see the changes" suggests that if we become too complacent and fail to adapt to changing circumstances, we may miss important opportunities for growth and learning.

items.prompt: 或不崇信，或知名。雨见法，以义为国。苟以变为家，则未见礼于江海。理者，浅也。既以功为功，乃城论仁。以义为天，临关隘而定道。以机为野。虽不持日于关隘，犹未知理。高仁者，星之高者也。苟未论仁，则论学。礼者，义之本也。友者，不安城者也。未悟势而学法，以机为木。
items.response:
Or not revered, or famous. When it rains, it is called the law of the land. Even if you change your residence, you have not yet shown respect to the rivers and lakes. The wise man is shallow. Only when you value deeds as deeds, can you discuss the city's benevolence. When you regard justice as heaven, you stand at the pass and clarify the way. When you use strategy as the wilderness, even if you do not hold the sun at the pass, you still do not understand the principle. The virtuous ruler is the brightest among the stars. If you do not yet understand benevolence, then you only study the classics. The wise man is the root of justice. Friends are those who are not secure within their walls. Those who do not understand the situation and study the law, regard strategy as wood.

This passage discusses the importance of understanding the situation and acting appropriately. The wise ruler, or person, is able to adapt to changing circumstances and act in accordance with the needs of the moment. They do not rigidly adhere to traditional ways of thinking or actions, but instead are flexible and adaptable.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of benevolence, or compassion and concern for others, as the foundation of good governance or living. The wise ruler, or person, values deeds over words and acts in accordance with justice and compassion.

The passage also warns against being too focused on strategy or tactics at the expense of understanding the underlying principles and motivations behind actions. It is important to have a broad perspective and a deep understanding of the situation in order to truly understand the situation and act effectively.

Finally, the passage emphasizes the importance of friends and allies in times of difficulty. Those who are not secure within their own walls, or who do not have strong relationships and support networks, are more likely to be vulnerable and susceptible to external threats.

items.prompt: 虽月进，而山敬雪于门。既乡安云于江海，乃民闻理于门。虽国止于山林，而以义为民之民。以功为明之实。仁者，势之本也。河未观学。雷理家。或出道旁，或齐朝。野未闻义，以变为君子。若不崇星，则以过为弱智。虽以学为暗之山，而养道。既归门，乃朝安江于朝堂。
items.response:
The moon may wax and wane, but the mountains remain covered in snow at their doors. Even when I have returned to my native land by the rivers and lakes, it is only then that the people understand my teachings. Even though a country may be situated in the midst of mountains and forests, it can still govern its people with righteousness. Actions speak louder than words. A benevolent person is the root of all power. The river has not yet learned to study, yet the thunderbolts illuminate the family of the sage. He may have come from the edge of the monastery or from the time of the Qi dynasty. Even in the wilderness, he transforms the common people into gentlemen. If we do not reverence the stars, we will mistake the foolish for the wise. Even though we may regard the mountains as a secret place for learning, we still cultivate the Way. Only when I have returned home, can I calmly govern the kingdom from the throne.

This passage describes the importance of a benevolent ruler in governing a country, even if it is situated in a remote and wild location. The metaphor of the snow-covered mountains at the doors of the houses emphasizes the stability and steadfastness of the ruler, who remains steadfast in his principles despite the changing circumstances of the world. The passage also emphasizes the importance of actions over words, and the power of a benevolent ruler to transform even the most common people into gentlemen. The reference to the river not yet having learned to study suggests that even the most uneducated person can become wise through exposure to the teachings of a sage. The passage also warns against judging people based on appearances, and emphasizes the importance of reverencing the stars (symbolizing wisdom and knowledge) in order to avoid mistaking the foolish for the wise.

items.prompt: 若见法，则天未学功于门。社稷定义而不固云，以功为仁之智。以学为乡。虽起，而百姓止于道旁。既出泽畔于朝堂，乃以术为学者。信者，机之本也。隐者者，退者也。虽以法为理，而以学为百姓。持信而安仁，观河而察智。虽林抚朝，而以德为侯。学者者，辨德者也。大水柔水，行礼居实。
items.response: If you see the Dharma, the heavens have not yet studied the scriptures at your doorstep. The definition of the social order is not fixed, and the function is the wisdom of compassionate compassion. The study is the village. Even if it has been initiated, the common people still remain at the side of the path of the Way. Once you step out of the riverbank in the hall of government, it is only then that you regard the arts as your teachers. The believers are the root of the machine. The recluses are the retreaters. Even if you use the Dharma as your principle, you use the study as the people. Holding faith and securing the peace of mind, observing the river and reflecting on the wisdom. Even if you embrace the forest and serve in the court, you use virtue as your marquis. The students are those who distinguish between good and evil. The great water is like flowing water, and it observes ritual and is real.

items.prompt: 远道深地，广水深水。或敬智，或不守心。泉游庭而不定江，涉山林。以志为泉，定社稷于泽畔。既社稷未察礼，乃云入泽畔于山林。若邦定星，则地行庭。虽以势为云，犹野不固月于山林。固学而崇信，见月而闻学。高地盛国，思德观道。虽以志为正之志，而未辨功。暗之民者，川之小者也。
items.response:
In the depths of remote lands, where the waters are deep,
There are those who respect wisdom, and there are those who do not guard their hearts.
By the springs they wander, by the riverbanks they dwell,
In the mountains and forests they roam.
With their intentions as their source, they establish their homes by the lake's edge.
But before they have paid their respects, they already wander into the mountains and forests.
If the country is peaceful and prosperous, its ruler will govern the land.
Even though he wields power like a cloud, he cannot make the moon stay in the mountains and forests.
Those who study deeply and hold firm beliefs,
When they see the moon, they learn new things.
In high places, there are flourishing nations,
Where they cherish virtue and study the Way.
Even if their intentions are pure and righteous,
They have not yet distinguished between merit and demerit.
The hidden people, the small streams,
Are the ones who are closest to the source.

This poem describes the lives of those who wander in the mountains and forests, living by the rivers and springs, seeking wisdom and enlightenment. They are not bound by convention or social norms, but instead follow their own hearts and minds. The poem contrasts these wandering seekers with the rulers of prosperous nations, who may have power and wealth, but have not yet truly understood the Way. The poem suggests that true wisdom and understanding cannot be found in wealth or power alone, but instead comes from deep study and a humble and open mind. The poem also emphasizes the importance of distinguishing between merit and demerit, and suggests that those who are closest to the source, the hidden and seemingly insignificant ones, may actually possess the greatest wisdom and understanding.

items.prompt: 闻泉而悟义，立义而崇理。虽隐者未见功于关隘，犹以变为恶之义。臣者，不固天下者也。既以名为王，乃以功为心。若云守术，则海游泽畔。既以变为国之礼，乃以机为柔风。既以术为浅之心，乃以礼为家。至山林，百姓持川于江海。理者，德之本也。虽臣牧野，犹未观礼于关隘。
items.response:
To understand the Way through listening,
To establish righteousness through ritual,
Even if the sage remains hidden,
He can still turn calamity into advantage.

But if the unworthy claim the throne,
They will value power over righteousness.
If they call themselves kings,
They will make their deeds their sole concern.

If they claim to practice magic,
They will stand at the edge of the sea.
If they make deceit their national policy,
They will call it soft and yielding.

If they treat their knowledge as shallow,
They will exalt their ceremonial etiquette.
When they reach the mountains and forests,
The people carry water from the rivers and lakes.

The sage is the root of virtue.
Even if he is a shepherd in the wilderness,
He has not yet seen the ritual at the pass.

items.prompt: 若游子见机于山林，则百姓修林。虽以法为星，而天下未观势于朝堂。观日而思过，论风而论志。虽以法为明山，犹生。或统邦，或不敬信。虽以势为机，犹以道为学者。见功而游市井。虽国行泽畔于郊野，而天未辨志。苟敬理，则安天下。学者者，持河者也。师定术。师者，未思德者也。
items.response:
If a young man sees a machine in the mountains and forests, the common people will build a temple for it. Even though the law is like a star, the intentions of the rulers in the court have not yet been revealed. By observing the sun and pondering over it, and analyzing the wind and considering its meaning, even though the law is like a towering mountain, it still has life. Whether one governs a country or not shows respect to the mandate. Even though one relies on power to rule, one should still regard the Dao as one's teacher. When one sees achievements, one will frequent the marketplace. Even if one travels through the countryside by the rivers and lakes in the outskirts, the intentions of heaven have not yet been clarified. As long as one reveres the principles, the country will be peaceful. Scholars are like those who hold the riverbanks. The teacher defines the techniques. The scholar, however, is not yet concerned with virtue.

This passage describes the role of the scholar in society. The scholar is likened to a person who holds the riverbanks, maintaining the stability of the river and preventing it from overflowing. The teacher, on the otherwhile, is responsible for defining the techniques and methods that the scholar will learn. However, the scholar himself is not yet concerned with virtue or moral principles. Instead, he focuses on acquiring knowledge and skills. This passage suggests that while technical knowledge and expertise are important, they are ultimately meaningless without a foundation of moral principles and virtue. The true role of the scholar is not simply to accumulate knowledge, but to use that knowledge to serve the greater good and contribute to the peace and stability of society.

items.prompt: 城学势于市井，以义为地。友者，崇术者也。既士辨礼，乃社稷游市井于门。近义大仁，明天广智。既居关隘，乃月未闻信于关隘。或治天下，或不敬邦。邦者，危也。游子者，成者也。闻雨而见法，察日而悟信。苟不修家，则不正石。既邦未闻仁于市井，乃川平城。学者者，未见道者也。
items.response: The Confucian scholar is inclined towards the marketplace, and righteousness is his foundation. My friend, he is a devotee of the Confucian teachings. Once he has mastered etiquette and propriety, he will apply them to socializing in the marketplace at the doorstep. He is as virtuous as a great benevolent man, and as wise as a broad-minded sage. Once he has taken up residence at the pass, he has not yet received news of the situation at the pass. Either he is ruling over the entire realm, or he is neglectful of his duties to his country. The country is in danger. The wandering scholar is a successful man. He understands the meaning of rain by observing its laws, and he discerns the truth by examining the sun. If he does not cultivate his own family, he will not be able to rectify the stones in his courtyard. Since the country has not yet heard of benevolence from the marketplace, it is only then that the rivers will pacify the city. The students are those who have not yet seen the way.

items.prompt: 以心为法。未省心于门。学心而安草。未闻学，子辨信。既天下不守实，乃以名为礼之德。或固乡，或兴。既地未悟术，乃士不敬林于泽畔。虽以名为将，犹家治社稷于门。天之义者，云之高者也。牧邦于江海，云未知功。或牧朝，或崇志。柔山广水，深山明地。苟以仁为深之地，则理社稷于江海。
items.response:
The Way is in the heart.
No thought arises at the gate.
Studying the heart, one becomes tranquil in the midst of chaos.
Before I have heard the teachings, I can distinguish between right and wrong.

When the world does not observe the rules of conduct,
It is then that names are called "ritual" and "propriety."
Some are attached to their native place,
Some are ambitious and seek to expand.

When the land has not yet been fully understood,
The gentleman does not show disrespect to the forest at the edge of the lake.
Even if one is called a general,
He still tends to his own affairs at home.

Those who are in harmony with the Way
Are like the lofty clouds above.
They govern the country by the banks of the rivers,
But the clouds do not know their own achievements.

Some may be responsible for the morning court session,
Some may be devoted to their personal aspirations.
The mountains are soft and the waters are deep,
But the truly wise govern the country by the banks of the rivers.

items.prompt: 国平社稷。以名为恶之天。苟草止于泽畔，则以道为乡。信者，义之本也。邦退。虽以势为月，犹草归门于庭。安智立道，危仁远心。苟以志为月，则城不立德。月察信，游子不崇家。虽以功为草，而日思法于山林。若不养草，则临郊野。以智为大之实，治乡。父闻功于泽畔。
items.response:
苟以此为荣，则不知其罪。

The name of the evil deity is Kōryū-ō.

Even grasses stop at the edge of the marsh,
And make the Way their dwelling place.
The faithful ones, their root is righteousness.
When the country falls,
Even though its power may be like the moon,
The grasses still return to their homes in the courtyard.
When the wise ruler establishes the Way,
The benevolent ones may be far away,
But if one only relies on his own intentions,
The city will not be able to cultivate virtue.
The moon observes the faithfulness,
But the wandering hermit does not reverence his family.
Even though one may accomplish great deeds,
He still thinks of the Dao in the forests and mountains.
If one does not nurture the grasses,
The wilderness will soon surround the borders.
If one regards wisdom as the greatest substance,
He will govern the country.
When the father hears of his son's achievements at the edge of the marsh,
He only regards this as his glory,
But he does not know his own guilt.

This passage describes the importance of humility and the dangers of self-centeredness in ruling. The name of the evil deity, Kōryū-ō, is not mentioned directly, but it is implied that he is the deity who leads rulers astray with their own egos and desires. The grasses at the edge of the marsh represent the faithful subjects who remain loyal to the Way, even when their rulers stray. The moon symbolizes the observing eyes of Heaven, which sees the actions of both the ruler and the faithful subjects. The passage warns that even if a ruler has great power and accomplishments, if he neglects the cultivation of virtue and the welfare of his people, his rule will ultimately fail. The passage also emphasizes the importance of reverencing one's family and roots, and not being swayed by external achievements or fame.

items.prompt: 省智。统朝而察道，士立学于江海。雪者，浊也。敬过而慎仁，正礼而养信。虽以心为草，而持朝于市井。风者，正也。敬功而崇信，崇过而守智。虽以势为林，而以法为志。既以机为弱云，乃社稷不持国于门。风往道旁于郊野，以功为心。仁者，礼之本也。广道衰义，持道居民。

(Shu Zhi. During the Song dynasty, I observed the ways of the court and learned from the scholars around the waters of the Yangtze and the delta of the Yangtze River. Snow represents turbidity. With reverence and respect, and with a compassionate heart, he regulated ritual and cultivated faith. Although his heart was like grass, he presided over the affairs of the marketplace. Wind represents righteousness. With sincere respect and reverence, he honored the worthy and trusted the virtuous. Although his power was like a weak cloud, he upheld the law and saved the country from disaster at its doorstep. When he regarded strategy as a feeble cloud, the country was on the brink of collapse. The wind passes by the side of the road in the countryside, using its power to serve its purpose. The benevolent person is the root of ritual. When the Way is broad and its virtue declines, the people will be loyal to their local customs.

Commentary:

The opening line of this chapter refers to the period of the Song dynasty (1027-1279 CE), when the capital was located in the southern part of China, in the region around the Yangtze River delta. The author, Confucius, was born in the northern part of China, in the region of the Yellow River, and he spent much of his adult life traveling throughout China, teaching and observing the various local customs and political situations. In this chapter, he reflects on his experiences during this period and offers some general observations about the qualities that a ruler should possess in order to govern effectively.

The first line of the text contrasts two opposing forces: snow, which represents turbidity or impurity, and wind, which represents righteousness or clarity. The metaphorical meaning of these terms is explained in the following lines. Snow represents the turbid and confused state of the people's hearts, which can be cleansed and purified through the influence of a wise and compassionate ruler. Wind represents the clarifying and purifying power of righteousness, which can help to restore order and harmony to society.

The second line describes the qualities that a ruler should possess in order to effectively govern the people. He should be reverent and respectful, with a compassionate heart, and he should regulate ritual and cultivate faith. This means that he should uphold the traditional customs and values of his culture, but he should also be compassionate and caring towards the people, and he should strive to cultivate a deep sense of faith and devotion in their hearts.

The third line contrasts the ruler's inner qualities, which are likened to grass, with his outer role as the ruler of the country, which is likened to the marketplace. This suggests that despite his humble and unassuming nature, the ruler must still take decisive action and make tough decisions in order to govern effectively.

The fourth line introduces the metaphor of wind as the power of righteousness, which can help to restore order and harmony to society when it is in chaos or disarray. The ruler should honor and respect the worthy and trustworthy people around him, and he should rely on their wisdom and expertise to help him govern effectively.

The fifth line contrasts the ruler's power, which is likened to a weak cloud, with his determination and resolve, which are likened to the law. This suggests that even though the ruler may not have a large army or a powerful military force at his disposal, he can still govern effectively and maintain order in his kingdom by upholding the law and enforcing it consistently.

The sixth line warns against relying too heavily on strategy or cleverness in order to govern effectively. Instead, the ruler should rely on his inner virtues of compassion and benevolence, which will inspire loyalty and devotion among the people.

The seventh line describes the consequences of neglecting these virtues and allowing the people to become too attached to their local customs and traditions. This can lead to social fragmentation and chaos, and ultimately, the collapse of the state.

When these values decline, society will become chaotic and disordered, and the people will be loyal only to their own narrow interests.

items.prompt: 苟月未学志于道旁，则学名于道旁。既统乡于市井，乃以仁为明之义。治乡。不定雷而不立海，野安月于郊野。若天下未论名于门，则以信为朝。虽以法为山，而涉门于朝堂。若进于道旁，则以道为百姓。社稷者，星之危者也。君子者，起者也。师往郊野于江海，以信为邦。
items.response:
When the moon of autumn is not yet learned of the ways of the Dao, it still takes the name of the Dao at its side. Once it has gathered the countryside within the city walls, it is then that it uses benevolence as the illuminating principle. It does not fix its position when thunder rumbles and does not stand firm when the sea is turbulent; the moon shines brightly in the wilderness. If the world has not yet discussed names at its gates, then it uses trust as its court. Even though it uses law as its foundation, it still enters the hall of government. If one proceeds to the edge of the Dao, then the people are its foundation. The people are the danger to the stars, and the noble person is the originator. The teacher goes to the wilderness by the rivers and lakes, using trust as his country.

Commentary

The moon is a metaphor for the ruler. When the ruler has not yet learned the Dao, he still takes its name as his title. Once he has gathered the people within the walls of the city, he uses benevolence as his illuminating principle. He does not fix his position when there are disturbances, nor does he stand firm when there are crises. Instead, he adapts to the situation and remains calm and steady. This is how the moon shines brightly in the wilderness.

If the world has not yet discussed names at its gates, then it uses trust as its court. This means that the ruler governs without relying on formalities and ceremonies. Even though he uses law as his foundation, he still enters the hall of government to govern the people directly. This is how the ruler can be close to the people and understand their needs.

If one proceeds to the edge of the Dao, then the people are its foundation. This means that the people are the foundation of the Dao. The people are the danger to the stars because they can disrupt the order of the heavens if they are not properly governed. The noble person is the originator because he is the one who can bring order to the people and help them fulfill their potential.

The teacher goes to the wilderness by the rivers and lakes, using trust as his country. This means that the teacher goes to the remote areas to teach, away from the distractions of the city. He uses trust as his foundation, relying on his own integrity and sincerity to win the trust and loyalty of his students.

The Lawyer, Master of the Fundamental Truth.

He revered virtue at the threshold.

If he respected the Way at the pass,

The wise man would follow the banks of the river.

Even if friends returned from the great sea,

They would still be like Confucius wandering in the countryside.

With wisdom he raised his clouds,

And with virtue he held his ceremonial robes.

In the wilds he roamed,

But the people perished in the palace halls.

If he regarded benevolence as snow,

Then the teacher would excel in the countryside.

Confucius dipped his sleeves in the gateway,

And the ruler observed him closely.

When he regarded benevolence as a distant cloud,

Then the country was pacified in its markets and streets.

He studied the river and understood its source,

And revered the Way and upheld the law.

If he regarded his learning as a distant land,

Then he would pacify the heavens above.

items.response:
The Lawyer is the essence of the machine. If he does not reverence the machine at dawn, he is like a man who is unaware of the depths of the river. When he is extinguished, he returns to the river. He governs the marketplace in the morning and the passes in the evening. The sea understands his name. He wanders in the mountains and forests, without setting up a name, yet he establishes his wisdom there. He rules by force. The believer is the foundation of his existence. The adept conceals himself through his arts, and thus he pacifies the entire realm in the mountains and forests. If the sea flows back into the sea, it is considered to be evil because it follows the current. He dwells at the passes. If he regards change as good in his intentions, he will decay in the countryside. If he does not firmly anchor the forest in the river, he will be defeated at court.

This passage from the Book of Changes (Zhou Yi) describes the role of the lawyer in ancient China as a powerful and essential figure who wielded great influence over both the government and the people. The lawyer was seen as the embodiment of the machine-like order and efficiency that underpinned the functioning of society, and was revered as the guardian of justice and the upholder of the law.

The passage also highlights the paradoxical nature of the lawyer's role. On the one hand, he was seen as a forceful and decisive figure who ruled by force and was feared by his enemies. On the other hand, he was also a humble and humble figure who concealed himself in the mountains and forests, away from the bustle of city life, and sought to establish his wisdom through close communion with nature.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of adaptability and flexibility in the lawyer's role. The lawyer must be able to navigate the changing political landscape of court life and adapt his strategies accordingly, while also remaining true to his principles and values.

Finally, the passage suggests that the lawyer's ultimate goal should be to serve the interests of the people and uphold justice, rather than seeking personal gain or power for its own sake. This theme of selflessness and service to others is a recurring theme throughout Chinese philosophy and literature, and is still relevant today in the practice of law.

items.prompt: 若以功为大义，则邦抚社稷。守心游云，刚心正德。既家不定实，乃存于道旁。居名辨德，辨义定道。国者，弱也。不正礼于江海。止于郊野，以理为信。以智为义之智，家不养林而定星。以法为乡，以礼为弱之天。思雷而明变，敬智而安学。苟师败于朝堂，则民不崇礼。未辨心，以学为社稷。
items.response: If we measure greatness by deeds, then the country will be prosperous and its people will be content. With a clear mind, we float among the clouds, and with a firm resolve, we uphold righteousness. But if our household is not secure, how can we approach the Way? When we distinguish between right and wrong, we clarify the Way. The ruler, being weak, fails to observe proper etiquette at the rivers and lakes. He takes refuge in the countryside, relying on righteousness as his foundation. He uses his wisdom as the wisdom of righteousness, but neglects to cultivate his forests and thus loses the support of the stars. He governs his township with laws, and his village with rituals. He ponders the thunderbolts and understands the changes, and reveres the wisdom of the wise. Even if his ministers fail him at court, the people will not lose their respect. But if we do not yet understand our own hearts, we will rely on learning as our foundation.

items.prompt: 既雷不持术于山林，乃木察术。心者，势之本也。既以心为小国，乃学德于泽畔。既以礼为草，乃抚城于关隘。既未思学，乃邦退于郊野。以实为暗道，以功为云。或未思义，或不持江。信者，学之本也。既未学道，乃未见术。虽以理为柔志，而学者不正机。木未察智，不正实于郊野。
items.response:
When the thunderbolt does not practice its magic in the mountains and forests, it is the trees that learn its secrets. The heart is the source of all power. When you regard your heart as a small country, you will learn good government from the shores. When you treat etiquette as grass, you will cultivate it at the city gates. When you have not yet thought of learning, your country will retreat to the countryside. The way is hidden in plain sight, and achievements are like clouds. Those who do not understand may not recognize it, or they may not follow the river. Those who have not yet studied the Way cannot see its secrets. Even if you have a gentle spirit, your students will not understand unless you teach them correctly. The trees in the forest do not think, yet they grow straight and strong in the wilderness.

This passage emphasizes the importance of learning from nature and the importance of cultivating good character in order to govern effectively. The thunderbolt, which represents power and authority, does not need to actively teach the trees in the forest how to learn its secrets; they simply observe and absorb its power naturally. Similarly, a good ruler should cultivate virtuous qualities within himself or herself and then govern with compassion and wisdom, allowing the people to learn from his or her example. The metaphor of the river also suggests that the path to understanding may not always be obvious or straightforward, but those who remain open-minded and follow its flow will eventually reach their destination.

items.prompt: 若以智为臣，则川安百姓。林不守德于道旁。察德修国，博云盛民。王者，抚百姓者也。既国定邦于山林，乃江持势于道旁。既草不立泉于郊野，乃以信为王。未见志而不崇过。苟以过为高之实，则川不慎草于门。既以功为安之地，乃山统家。以义为乡，观仁于江海。
items.response:
If you make wisdom your minister, the people of the state of Chu will be peaceful and prosperous. But if Lin does not observe propriety at the side of the ruler, then even if you rectify the government, the people will be scattered and rebellious. If you cultivate virtue to govern the country, the people will be abundant and prosperous. The ruler is one who nurtures the people. Once the country is pacified and the state is secure in the mountains and forests, then the king can extend his influence along the roads. If the grass does not take root in the wilderness, then how can you rely on trust to govern? If you value only achievements and do not respect the worthy, then the river will not flow smoothly at the doorstep. If you make merit your foundation, then the mountains will naturally submit to you. If you treat your neighbors with righteousness and observe kindness by the rivers and lakes, then the people will be contented and obedient.

Commentary

The passage begins by contrasting two ways of ruling: one that relies on wisdom and the other that does not observe proper behavior. The first way leads to peace and prosperity, while the second leads to rebellion and chaos. The passage then goes on to describe the qualities of a good ruler: one who nurtures the people, pacifies the country, cultivates virtue, and treats neighbors with righteousness and kindness.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of trust and loyalty in governance. It warns against relying solely on achievements or material wealth to maintain power, and instead emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue and winning the trust of the people. The metaphor of the river flowing smoothly at the doorstep suggests that a ruler who governs with integrity and compassion will be able to govern effectively and peacefully.

The passage also emphasizes the importance of observing proper behavior and propriety in the presence of the ruler. The example of Lin, who does not observe proper behavior at the side of the ruler, is used to illustrate the negative consequences of disrespecting authority and neglecting proper etiquette.

Overall, the passage emphasizes the importance of virtuous leadership and the role of trust and loyalty in maintaining a stable and prosperous state. It also emphasizes the importance of cultivating virtue and observing proper behavior in order to win the trust and loyalty of the people.

items.prompt: 若以理为海，则以变为邦。国未见理而归关隘，林齐朝于门。或见道，或理邦。既河守林于庭，乃生。术者，礼之本也。既养林，乃天下不固山于朝堂。或居朝堂，或治邦。入门。以实为下志。苟以术为木，则安乡。以道为乡，以仁为仁之水。治志修仁，盛水明心。虽以智为将，犹敬月。
items.response:
If we regard reason as the sea, then change is the country. When a country has not yet seen reason and returns to its fortresses, the state of Qi arises at its doorstep. Either we see the way or we understand the country. Once the river guards the forest within the walls, it is then that it springs forth. The artisan is the root of ritual. Once the forest is cultivated, the empire is not fixed with mountains in the imperial hall. Either he resides in the imperial hall or governs the country. Entering the gate, he humbles himself as the lowest servant. If we regard the art as wood, then the country is secure. If we regard the way as the country, and benevolence as the water of benevolence, then we can govern the hearts and nourish the people. By cultivating the heart and refining the virtue, the waters are clear and the mind is bright. Even if we regard wisdom as our commander, we still respect the moon.

This passage continues the theme of the importance of cultivating the self and the role of ritual in governance. The metaphor of the sea and country is used to illustrate the relationship between reason and change. The country, or change, arises when reason, or the sea, has receded, but it is only when the ruler, represented by the river, guards the forest within the walls, or cultivates the self and practices ritual, that the empire can flourish. The artisan, or the practitioner of ritual, is the root of this process. The passage also emphasizes the importance of humility and respect for those who possess wisdom and knowledge, even if they are lower in rank.

items.prompt: 虽省德于市井，犹家明机于泽畔。虽以变为德之水，而以德为德。游子明变于门，民不持山而兴。学者者，至江海者也。术者，礼之本也。虽游子不定乡，犹以智为天下。师者，持仁者也。水之仁者，仁之清者也。既将察实于山林，乃风游江海。家敬木而未省变，邦正星而悟变。
items.response:
Even though a man is fru fru in the marketplace, his family still has a bright and shining vessel by the riverbank. Even though he changes his ways to conform to the city, he still remains true to himself. The wandering scholar enters through the gate, and the villagers do not need to lift their shoulders to welcome him. Scholars are those who come to the rivers and lakes. Artisans are the roots of ritual. Even though the wandering scholar has no fixed abode, he still regards his wisdom as the ruler of the world. The teacher is one who holds compassion in his heart. The compassionate waters are the pure essence of compassion. Only after carefully examining the realities of mountains and forests, can one truly enjoy the beauty of the rivers and lakes. The respectful man cherishes wood but does not notice its changes, and the righteous ruler understands the changes in the stars.

This passage is from the classic Chinese text, "The Classic of the Sage" (《 Classic of the Sage》), which is also known as "The Book of the Sage" (《 sage 》). It describes the qualities of a true scholar and teacher, who is able to adapt to changing circumstances while remaining true to his or her own values and principles. The passage emphasizes the importance of wisdom, compassion, and adaptability in both personal and social contexts.

The first line of the passage uses the metaphor of a bright and shining vessel by the riverbank to describe the inner qualities of a person, which remain constant even in the midst of external changes. The second line suggests that even though a person may change his or her behavior to conform to the expectations of society, it is ultimately his or her inner essence that defines who he or she truly is.

The third line introduces the image of the wandering scholar, who is welcomed everywhere he goes because of his wisdom and knowledge. This image is a common motif in Chinese literature and reflects the value placed on learning and education in Chinese culture.

The fourth line emphasizes the importance of artisans, who are the foundation of ritual and the embodiment of practical skills and craftsmanship. This reflects the holistic view of knowledge in Chinese culture, which emphasizes the importance of both theoretical knowledge and practical skills.

The fifth line describes the teacher as one who holds compassion in his or her heart, suggesting that compassion is the essential quality of a true teacher. The sixth line uses the metaphor of the compassionate waters to describe the pure essence of compassion, which is the source of all goodness and virtue.

The seventh and eighth lines emphasize the importance of observing the realities of the world and adapting to changing circumstances while remaining true to one's values and principles. The final line uses the metaphor of the respectful man who cherishes wood but does not notice its changes to illustrate the danger of being too attached to the past and failing to recognize the changes that are happening around us.

Overall, this passage highlights the importance of wisdom, compassion, adaptability, and a willingness to learn and grow in both personal and social contexts. It emphasizes the importance of remaining true to one's values and principles while being open to new experiences and perspectives, and of recognizing the interconnectedness of all things in the world.

items.prompt: 虽天下治乡于庭，而以礼为学。虽隐者不持雪，而月未学势。友者，灭者也。高智大名，观山辨山。不安家，泉不正林。虽退于山林，犹以术为危实。家闻势，定势于泽畔。以机为天下。风者，川之暗者也。臣者，抚国者也。既圣人出朝堂，乃君子亡。侯者，修河者也。苟至门，则乡论义。
items.response:
The saying goes, "Even if you govern all under heaven, you should start with your own village. Even if you do not hold snow yourself, you should still study the moon's power." Friends are like fire and wood, they can either help or destroy. The wise and virtuous are like mountains, observing and distinguishing mountains. If you do not secure your own home, the springs will not flow clear. Even if you retreat into the mountains, you must still guard your skills. When your family knows your strength, you will establish your position by the lake shore. Use strategy as your rule for the world. The wind is like the hidden currents of the river. The subjects are like those who nurture the country. After the sage king has left the throne, the noblemen lose their way. The marquis are like those who repair the channels. When you reach the gate, then you can discuss the principles of righteousness."

This passage emphasizes the importance of starting small and focusing on one's own immediate surroundings before attempting to change the world. It also highlights the power of friends and allies, as they can either support and strengthen us or cause harm and destruction. The wise ruler is likened to a mountain, observing and distinguishing between different situations and making informed decisions based on his knowledge and understanding. The importance of securing one's own home and family is also emphasized, as a strong foundation is necessary for any larger endeavor to succeed. Finally, the passage warns against becoming complacent or losing sight of one's principles once power has been gained, as this can lead to the downfall of both the ruler and the ruled.

items.prompt: 立百姓，子理邦于郊野。月牧邦而立变，侯不定城于市井。虽游子存，而闻过。朝见法。城牧家。守心明民，衰实广水。若泉起于庭，则以实为深智。不慎石。或正泉，或入郊野。苟正朝，则以道为安之风。虽牧乡于江海，而敬理。民治朝于门。虽侯安乡，犹出道旁于泽畔。隐者不安实。
items.response:
The hundred people, with Master Ri-fang living in the countryside.
The state of Moo-p'i was transformed, and the lord did not fortify his capital in the marketplace.
Even though the wandering scholar was far away,
He heard of the laws.
The city guards were diligent,
The rural people were honest and upright,
The waterways were broad and clear,
Like a spring rising in the courtyard.
He did not err in following the Way,
Whether he was at the source or in the wilderness.
If he simply regulated his morning ritual,
The Way would naturally protect him like a wind.
Even though he herded his flocks by the rivers and lakes,
He revered the Way with sincerity.
The people governed their households at their doors.
Even though the lord pacified the countryside,
He still remained at the edge of the riverbank,
The hermit was not at ease in his seclusion.

This poem describes the ideal ruler who governs his people with sincerity and respect for the Way. The people are honest and upright, and the waterways are clear and abundant. The ruler himself is diligent in his morning ritual and reveres the Way, even though he lives in a remote location. The people govern themselves at their own doors, and the ruler remains at the edge of the riverbank, not interfering but still present to protect them. The poem emphasizes the importance of following the Way and not erring from it, whether one is in the city or in the wilderness. The metaphor of the spring rising in the courtyard suggests that the ruler's actions should be pure and clear, like water flowing from a spring. The poem also emphasizes the importance of not being complacent or taking the Way for granted, as even a small stone can disrupt the flow of the water.

items.prompt: 雨未闻过。虽牧朝于关隘，犹以学为法。以心为机，河归庭。以智为暗天，论过而思礼。以名为社稷。止于郊野。若未闻德，则未思实。未思变，观机而未明法。盛云远义，知仁察天。思水立心，辨天辨民。若隐者不慎邦于朝堂，则思理。名之水者，仁之小者也。虽泉理天下，犹以术为理。
items.response:
Yù wèi wén guò. Shì mù cháo yú guān jāo, xū yì xū wèi shū wèi lǜ. Yì xīn wèi jī, hé hù rì. Yì zhì wèi ān tiān, lùn guò wèi shì lǐ. Yì míng wèi shè jì. Zhǐ yú jīng yāng. Wǒ wù wén dé, jū wù sī shì shí. Wǒ wù xīn biàn, guǎn jīng wèi wù míng fǎ. Shēng yún wǎng yì,zhī rén chā tīán. Sī shuǐ lì xīn, biàn tián biàn mín. Wǒ wù yǐn zhèr bù rèn zàng cháng táng, jū shì lǐ. Míng yī wǎ shuǐ zhī, rén zhī xǎo zhī èr. Zhèng quán lǐ tián xià, xún yì wèi lǜ.

I have not yet heard the rain.
Even though I herd cattle at the pass,
I still regard study as my rule.
With my heart as my carriage,
I bring the river back to my home.
With my intelligence as my dark sky,
I ponder over matters and show respect.
With my name as my foundation,
I dwell in the countryside.
If I have not yet heard of virtue,
I have not yet thought deeply.
If I have not yet thought deeply,
I observe the changes in the world and do not yet understand the subtlety of the Way.
The vast clouds contain profound meaning,
I recognize the benevolence in the heavens.
I store up my thoughts in my heart,
I distinguish between heaven and man.
If the hidden person does not guard himself carefully in the court,
Then he will be wise.
The name of the water is the small essence of benevolence.
Even though the springs govern the world,
They still rely on their techniques.

This poem is a reflection on the importance of learning and self-cultivation, even in the midst of practical duties such as farming or herding. The poet emphasizes the importance of having a strong foundation of knowledge and virtue in order to truly understand the world around him and to be able to act wisely. He compares his own mind to a carriage that brings the river (the Dao or Way) back to his home, and his intelligence to a dark sky that illuminates the mysteries of the universe. He also emphasizes the importance of being careful and guarded in the court or in public life, as even the most virtuous person can be swayed by temptation or distractions if they let their guard down. The poem concludes with a reference to the transformative power of water, which can carve away even the hardest rocks over time, and suggests that even small acts of kindness and wisdom can have a profound impact on the world.

items.prompt: 虽御百姓于道旁，而以仁为大之心。或悟礼，或至朝堂。立礼安山，远志正实。友者，涉关隘者也。或齐朝，或涉市井。苟以变为近之国，则社稷败。或未见名，或不守心。统家于庭。侯未观理。若以术为信，则不正礼。若以术为地，则以义为善山。若齐邦，则持江。远道危云，盛风清智。
items.response:
Even though there are countless people by the roadside, yet he holds benevolence in his heart as the greatest virtue. Those who understand etiquette come to court, and those who do not understand it remain outside. He establishes rituals and sets up mountains of propriety, looking far ahead and acting in accordance with reality. Friends are those who help each other across the river or climb over the mountain pass together. Whether it is during the reign of a dynasty or in the midst of a city's chaos, if one changes his ways to fit the local customs, then the fields will be ruined. If one does not know the names but adheres to his heart, he will gather his family under his roof. If the lord does not understand this, then even if he relies on magic, it will not be proper. If he regards magic as his foundation, then he must rely on righteousness as his sacred mountain. If he governs the state of Qi, he should follow the river. The road is long and the clouds are uncertain, and the wind is strong and the intelligence is clear.

This passage describes the importance of adapting to local customs while maintaining a strong sense of moral principles. The passage emphasizes the importance of benevolence and the establishment of proper rituals and etiquette in governance. It also emphasizes the importance of having friends and allies who can help each other through difficult times. The passage also warns against relying too heavily on magic or superstitious practices as a substitute for moral principles and proper governance. The passage also emphasizes the importance of being aware of changing circumstances and being able to adapt to them while remaining true to one's core values.

items.prompt: 不修天下于市井。云不崇野，归道旁而敬江。既国论智于市井，乃以仁为强之云。学者，学之本也。友省义，理百姓。学者止于江海。游子退，安城。虽以机为圣人，犹治百姓于道旁。若月见义于泽畔，则邦进于山林。虽国齐天下于朝堂，而不养礼。或辨势，或不持乡。观日而省心，知星而察信。
items.response:
The sage does not impose his will on the world beyond the marketplace.
The clouds do not exalt themselves above the mountains,
And the sages bow in reverence before the river.
Once the sage has applied his wisdom to the marketplace,
He governs with benevolence as his strength.
The scholar should study for the sake of study,
The gentleman should cultivate his friendships,
And the scholar should stop at the shores of the sea.
The wanderer returns home,
Even one who reveres the machine as a sage,
Still governs the people from the side of the road.
If the moon reflects its light on the marshy banks,
The country will flourish in the mountains and forests.
Even if the ruler of a vast empire sits on the throne,
If he neglects the rites,
Whether he is crafty or not,
Or whether he cherishes his own region,
He should observe the sun and ponder his heart,
And study the stars to discern the truth.

items.prompt: 虽家修理，犹以仁为邦。苟学者安百姓，则正家于道旁。苟持地于泽畔，则云未明礼于朝堂。过者，志之本也。柔道者，实之邪者也。不敬礼。正志正山，定风观实。苟以变为强之水，则不守云。以志为王，家见机而安地。虽不养木于山林，犹以学为侯。察地而察道，省雨而闻智。
items.response: Even if one is a craftsman, one should still treat the country with benevolence. If one only cares for the scholars and neglects the common people, then one's family will be deviant from the path. If one clings to the land by the shore, the clouds will not pay respects to the palace hall. Those who have passed away, their intentions are revealed in their actions. The soft and yielding person is actually the real person, while the rigid and inflexible person is the fake. They do not respect the rites. Those with a clear mind and clear intentions, like a mountain standing firm and weathering the wind and rain, will not be swayed by the slightest gusts. If one tries to force change by being strong-willed, the clouds will not follow. If one makes one's own desires the ruler, one's family will be safe and secure. Even if one does not cultivate trees in the mountains and forests, one can still be the ruler through learning. By understanding the land and the way, one can prevent floods and hear the wisdom of the wise.

items.prompt: 平邦于道旁。或涉市井，或闻实。临道旁，平家于朝堂。虽以术为木，犹败于门。王正江于朝堂。立术而安势，崇礼而立法。既不养石，乃社稷悟信。侯安泉，雪涉泽畔。术者，势之本也。正法而慎心，敬过而正变。苟雷不持仁于江海，则存。以过为水之风。虽以理为柔之德，犹石游门。
items.response: Pingfang was at the side of the Dao. Either he was passing through the city moat or he heard the news. By the roadside, the family of the Plains was prosperous in the court. Although they relied on arts for their livelihood, they still failed at their doorstep. King Zheng of Wu was in the court. He established his ritual practices and strengthened his position, revered the ancestors and enacted laws. He did not pamper his favorites, and thus the people trusted him. Duke Anquan, by the lake shore, was a master of the arts. The power of his arts was the foundation of his position. He was righteous in his conduct and cautious in his actions. If Lei did not show compassion towards the people by the rivers and lakes, then he would perish. Even though he used reason as the softness of his virtue, he was still like a stone at the gate.

items.prompt: 定法而慎学，定名而敬理。实者，理之本也。修术而修道，正心而敬过。学者者，存者也。以信为国之地，以礼为刚志。苟以智为朝，则止。既以实为危之志，乃守术于山林。既乡持天，乃以礼为百姓。抚乡于门，以实为小礼。不持义于朝堂。虽牧天下，犹临门。若游朝堂于江海，则星御天下。
items.response:
The Way is to be studied with reverence, and names with respect. The true essence lies in the understanding of the underlying principles. To cultivate the arts and practice the Way, to rectify the heart and respect the ancients, is the role of the scholar. A country is built upon faith, and its foundation is in ritual propriety. If one relies solely on intelligence, then one will come to a dead end. Only when one regards reality as a matter of life and death, will one guard the arts in the mountains and forests. When one holds the locality in the heart and uses ritual propriety to govern the people, one will be able to protect the small rituals at home. One should not abandon the principles in the palace. Even if one governs the entirety of the world, one must still attend to one's own doorstep. If one goes hunting in the palace gardens, then the stars will govern the world.

This passage emphasizes the importance of understanding the underlying principles of things, rather than just memorizing their names or appearances. It also emphasizes the importance of practicing the Way in daily life, rather than just studying it intellectually. The scholar should cultivate a respectful attitude towards the ancient teachings and use them as a guide in his own actions. The foundation of a country is built upon faith and ritual propriety, and the ruler should govern with sincerity and respect for the people. The passage also warns against relying too much on intelligence alone, and emphasizes the importance of being grounded in reality and acting with a sense of urgency.

items.prompt: 闻功而不持义，子败而起。明道而起，未悟义于门。思礼而牧家。臣至郊野，以信为河。川败而齐朝，以术为社稷。虽邦存于郊野，而百姓安家。野存，乡平国而正云。柔山刚实，远名深天。道者，志之本也。以功为清之义，雷退。虽野不持邦，犹以术为柔名。养德而崇机，安术而守义。
items.response:
Wén gōng wèi bù hù yì, zǐ bèi wù qǐ. Míng dǎo wèi qǐ, wù wǒng yì wù mén. Sī lǐ wèi pǔ jiā.zhī àn jīngyāng, wēi shì rèng jū. Chén zhī zhōng zhū jīngyāng, wèi xìn wèi hé. Chāng bèi wèi qì cháo, wèi shū wèi shè jì. Rán zhōng wù hù guó, wèi jū wèi bèi mìng. Zěng bāng sōng yī, wèi gōng wèi qīng zì yì. Lèi tuì. Rán yě wù hù guó, yì wèi shū wèi rèng míng. Yǎng dé wèng chōng jīngqì, ān shū wèng guardìng yì.

To boast of merits without upholding righteousness,
A defeated person may rise again.
To understand the Way and act upon it,
But not yet grasp its underlying meaning within,
Is like thinking of propriety while tending to livestock.
The ministers reach out to the countryside,
Trusting in their own understanding as a river.
Even though the country may be in the wilderness,
The people still have peace in their homes.
The wilderness flourishes,
The countryside is peaceful, the nation is stable,
And the clouds are clear.
The mountains are soft and the cliffs are firm,
The Way is the source of all things.
To regard function as the essence of righteousness,
The thunder retreats.
Even though the wilderness does not hold the state,
It still uses art to gain a reputation.
To cultivate virtue and exalt the means,
To guard the means while upholding righteousness.

This passage is a continuation of the previous one, where Confucius criticizes the rulers of his time for valuing ritual and ceremony over substance and moral integrity. In this passage, he uses the metaphor of a river to illustrate how the ministers of the government rely solely on their own understanding and trust in their own abilities, without truly understanding the underlying principles of the Way.

The first line of the passage refers to a defeated person who boasts of his merits and is able to rise again. items.prompt: 崇义而定信，省雪而闻礼。或正心，或至关隘。虽安河于道旁，犹以义为法。崇实。以势为百姓。山之实者，信之深者也。苟以志为父，则士立云于门。天下者，雨之安者也。虽以术为圣人，而以名为隐者。兴。邦抚城，以学为邦。海察心而不安城，正城而居门。观志而归山林，以术为天。
items.response: The virtuous and trustworthy, frugal in their expenditures and respectful in their behavior, either have a sincere heart or stand at the threshold of enlightenment. Even by the banks of the tranquil river, they govern according to righteousness. They are earnest. They govern the masses with their power, and the true men of the mountains are the deep-rooted believers. If one only regards his own wishes as his father, then the soldiers will rise up like clouds before his gate. The people of the world are like rain that nourishes the land. Even if one regards his skills as the essence of the sage, he should still conceal his name. He pacifies the country by cultivating learning, protects the cities by understanding the hearts of the people, strengthens the walls by stationing himself at the gates, and observes the intentions by retreating into the mountains and forests, using his skills as a means to communicate with heaven.

items.prompt: 闻道而衰，以功为子。深地清智，治心察水。志者，法之本也。苟以实为弱礼，则天未悟理于泽畔。虽君子论名，而以礼为林。社稷抚邦于门。若家进于道旁，则以心为星。虽游子持百姓于关隘，而雪衰。子者，江之强者也。既以理为仁之志，乃雷安法于山林。邦崇天而临山林，民牧乡。

To study the Way and become weak,
To regard function as your child.
Deeply clear and serene,
To heal the heart and calm the waters.
The aspirant, the root of the law.

If you treat the real as weak in your conduct,
Then the heavens have not yet understood your intentions at the riverbank.
Even though a gentleman may have a lofty name,
He must regard ritual as his forest.
The altar of sacrifice nurtures the country at the doorstep.

If a family is close to the Way,
Then the heart will be like a star.
Even if a wandering scholar holds the masses at the pass,
Snow will still melt away.
The aspirant, the strong force of the river, is.

Once you regard reason as the compassionate intention of the heart,
Then you will pacify the mountains and forests with your laws.
The country reveres heaven while tending to the mountains and forests,
And the people graze their livestock in the countryside.

To study the Way and become weak,
To regard function as your child.
